# The Discipline Pill

I've been noticing something strange on TV lately.

I'm forced now to watch a lot of streaming with ads, so I like to keep track of what's being sold to me. Call it professional curiosity. And over the past month or so, something has shifted. The ad mix used to be the usual suspects: insurance, cars, phone plans, prescription medications for ailments I've never heard of.

Now? It's weight loss drugs. Everywhere.

Not just one company. Not just one brand. Five, six, seven different companies, all pushing their version of the same basic molecule. Different names, different spokespeople, different jingles. But the same promise: take this injection, lose the weight, change your life.

I'd estimate the shift from maybe 5% of ads being weight loss drugs to something like 80%. In a month. Maybe two.

What happened?

## The Patent Cliff

The patents started expiring.

Semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, has been under Novo Nordisk's exclusive control since it hit the market. At $1,000+ per month, it was a drug for the wealthy, the well-insured, or the desperate. Hollywood's open secret. The reason celebrities kept showing up thirty pounds lighter with suspiciously vague explanations about "lifestyle changes."

But starting in 2026, the protection is crumbling. [Canada's patent lapsed](https://fortune.com/2025/06/17/novo-nordisk-ozempic-wegovy-semaglutide-canada-patent-protection-fee/) (hilariously, because Novo Nordisk failed to pay a $450 maintenance fee). China, India, Brazil, Turkey are all opening up. Generic manufacturers are circling. Compounding pharmacies are filling the gap with their own versions.

The floodgates are opening, and the pharmaceutical companies can smell the blood in the water. Hence the ad blitz. Everyone wants to establish their brand before the market gets crowded.

What was once a whispered solution for the privileged is about to become as common as coffee.

## The Adjacent Advertising

It's not just the drug ads themselves. It's everything adjacent.

I saw a Wayfair commercial the other day featuring Kelly Clarkson. She's lost a significant amount of weight over the past year. The ad wasn't *about* weight loss. It was about furniture. But there she was, noticeably transformed, selling me couches.

This is the new normal. Celebrities who've clearly used GLP-1s, appearing everywhere, their transformed bodies serving as implicit advertisements even when they're selling something else entirely. You don't need to see the drug commercial when the drug's results are walking around in every other commercial.

And look, I'm not here to shame anyone for their choices. But let's be honest about what we're seeing. These transformations aren't happening because celebrities suddenly discovered discipline they didn't have before. They're happening because they had early access to expensive medication, and now they're everywhere, modeling a standard of appearance that will soon be chemically available to everyone.

What does that do to us? What does it mean when the before/after is everywhere, but the method is invisible?

## What the Drug Actually Does

Let's talk about what GLP-1 agonists actually do to your body. Because it's weirder than you think.

The simple explanation is that they suppress appetite. You take the drug, you feel less hungry, you eat less, you lose weight. Calories in, calories out. Simple.

Except that's not really what happens.

GLP-1 drugs don't just make you less hungry. They change what you *want*. They work directly on your brain's reward system, altering how you experience pleasure from food. Users describe it as silencing "food noise", that constant background chatter of cravings and urges and negotiations with yourself about what you're going to eat.

For people who've never experienced this, it's hard to describe. Imagine your brain is a radio tuned to a station that's always playing food advertisements. GLP-1s turn down the volume. Sometimes they turn it off entirely. It's the more expensive tier of streaming services with no ads.

Oprah Winfrey, who has been spokeswoman for failed weight loss fads over the years, jumped on this particular bandwagon now for these drugs. She says, "One of the things that I realized the very first time I took a GLP-1 was that, oh, thin people aren't just exercising more willpower. They're not even *thinking* about it. They're only eating when they're hungry, and they're stopping when they're full."

That's the revelation. The thin person's experience of food is fundamentally different from the heavy person's experience. It's not about discipline. It's about whether your brain is constantly screaming at you to eat.

And now there's a drug that can give you the thin person's brain.

But the food noise is only part of the story. [GLP-1s work on multiple biological systems simultaneously](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11944337/). They slow gastric emptying, so food sits in your stomach longer and you feel full for hours after eating. They improve insulin regulation, smoothing out the blood sugar spikes and crashes that trigger hunger. They appear to actively mobilize stored fat, particularly the visceral fat around organs. Some research suggests they may even increase basal metabolic rate.

In other words: the drug doesn't just give you a thin person's relationship with cravings. It gives you something closer to a thin person's entire metabolic system.

People on GLP-1s don't just lose their appetite for food. [Many of them lose their appetite for other addictive behaviors](https://sph.brown.edu/news/2025-07-24/brain-science-glp-1s-addiction). Alcohol. Nicotine. Gambling. Online shopping. Nail biting.

This suggests something profound and a little unsettling. These drugs aren't just targeting hunger. They're targeting *craving itself*. The reward circuitry in your brain that makes you want things you know you shouldn't have. The voice that says "one more" and "just this once" and "you deserve it."

What does it mean to pharmacologically silence that voice? Is that freedom? Or is it something else?

## The Set Point

Before we go further, I need to tell you about a framework that's been around since the 1960s. It's called [Health at Every Size](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_at_Every_Size), or HAES.

HAES emerged partly from an observation that kept showing up in the data: diets don't work. Not in the sense that people can't lose weight. They can. The problem is they can't keep it off. Study after study showed the same pattern. People would diet, lose weight, stop dieting, and regain the weight. Often more than they lost. [UCLA researchers found](https://newsroom.ucla.edu/releases/Dieting-Does-Not-Work-UCLA-Researchers-7832) that the majority of people regained all the weight, plus more, and that the failure rate for long-term weight loss through diet and exercise alone hovers somewhere between 80% and 95%, depending on how you measure it.

The HAES framework proposed an explanation. Your body has a "set point," a weight range it defends through biological mechanisms you don't consciously control. Hunger hormones. Metabolic adaptation. The food noise Oprah described. Gastric emptying rates. Insulin response. How readily your body releases or holds onto fat. When you diet, you're not just fighting your habits. You're fighting your biology. And biology almost always wins.

The conclusion HAES drew from this was radical but logical. If the war is unwinnable, stop fighting it. Focus instead on healthy behaviors, eating intuitively, moving joyfully, regardless of what the scale says. Your worth isn't your weight. Health isn't a single number.

This framework spawned what we now call the body positivity movement. The slow expansion of fashion sizes. The pushback against diet culture. All of it flows from HAES's central empirical claim: your body wants to be a certain size, and fighting that is both futile and harmful.

I think HAES got something important right, even if it does spell almost certain doom for your New Years resolution. The cruelty of telling people they just need to try harder, when biology is stacking the deck against them, is real. The psychological damage of yo-yo dieting is real. The way we moralize weight, treating thinness as virtue and fatness as failure, causes genuine harm.

But GLP-1s have thrown a wrench into the whole framework.

## The War Becomes Winnable

Here's the thing about GLP-1s. They don't refute HAES's empirical observation. They confirm it.

[Oprah's story](https://www.hola.com/us/celebrities/20251231875646/oprah-winfrey-when-stopped-using-glp-1-weight-loss-drugs/) is instructive because she actually tried to quit. She started taking GLP-1s in 2023. Less than six months later, on her 70th birthday, she stopped cold turkey. She wanted to prove she could do it without the drug. That the weight loss she'd achieved was really *hers*.

"I tried to beat the medication," she admitted recently.

It didn't work.

Despite maintaining her rigorous exercise routine, despite eating carefully, the weight came back. She gained eight pounds. Not a lot, but enough to understand something important: the drug wasn't teaching her body to behave differently. It was overriding multiple systems at once: appetite, digestion, fat storage, maybe metabolism itself. And when the override was removed, every system snapped back to its default.

"It's going to be a lifetime thing," she said. She compared it to blood pressure medication. "If I go off the high blood pressure medication, my blood pressure is going to go up. The same thing is true with these medications."

This is HAES's set point theory in action. Oprah's body wanted to return to its baseline. The moment the chemical override was removed, biology took back the wheel.

[Between half and two-thirds of people](https://news.northwestern.edu/stories/2024/11/why-do-50-75-of-people-stop-taking-glp-1-drugs-within-a-year) who start GLP-1s stop taking them within a year, for various reasons: side effects, cost, the belief that they don't need it anymore. And the weight comes back. Not because they're weak. Because the drug was doing something their body couldn't do on its own, and when you remove the drug, the body returns to its default state.

So HAES was right about the biology. But HAES's practical conclusion, "stop fighting, accept your body," was partly built on the premise that you *couldn't* win. The war was unwinnable, so make peace.

What happens when the war becomes winnable? Not through willpower, but through pharmacy.

## Rich People Get Ozempic

In May 2024, South Park released a special called "The End of Obesity." Because of course they did.

In it, Cartman tries to get Ozempic but can't afford it. His insurance won't cover it. So his doctor prescribes him an alternative: "Lizzo."

"She's a really good singer who talks about body positivity and just being happy with the way you look. I want you to listen to Lizzo five times a day, and watch her videos just before bedtime. I'm afraid you'll have to be on Lizzo for the rest of your life."

The episode included a fake commercial for "Lizzo" as a medication: "FDA-approved Lizzo makes you feel good about your weight, and it costs 90% less than Ozempic. In case studies, 70% of patients on Lizzo no longer cared how much they weighed."

Cartman's response cuts to the heart of it: "Rich people get Ozempic. Poor people get body positivity."

It's brutal. It's also uncomfortably close to true.

(By the way...[Lizzo has since admitted](https://www.billboard.com/music/music-news/lizzo-says-tried-ozempic-diet-change-weight-loss-1236004195/) she tried GLP-1s, though she says she stopped and found another approach.)

The South Park joke works because it exposes a tension that body positivity advocates haven't fully reckoned with. If accepting your body was always the psychologically healthy choice, independent of whether you *could* change it, then the availability of GLP-1s shouldn't matter. You'd still choose acceptance.

But if body positivity was partly a coping mechanism for an unwinnable war, a way to make peace with something you couldn't change, then the availability of a way *out* changes everything. The acceptance wasn't about acceptance. It was about resignation dressed up as empowerment.

I don't think that's entirely fair to HAES. The framework's deeper claims, that your worth isn't your weight, that health behaviors matter more than scale numbers, that we shouldn't moralize body size, those still stand regardless of what drugs exist.

But the practical advice? "Stop dieting, it doesn't work"? That lands differently when dieting still doesn't work but there's now a pill that does.

## The Promise

Let me tell you what the ads are really selling.

It's not weight loss. Weight loss is the mechanism. What they're selling is transformation. A new life. The implicit promise that once you have the body, everything else falls into place. Confidence. Attractiveness. Success. Happiness. The before/after photos tell a story, and the story is: that person became this person, and this person is clearly living a better life.

This promise isn't new. It's the same promise diet culture has been making for decades. Lose the weight, and you'll finally be the person you were meant to be. You'll finally be worthy. You'll finally be loved.

GLP-1s just make the promise seem more achievable. The old methods failed, but this one works. So the logic goes: if the only thing standing between you and happiness was an uncooperative body, and now the body cooperates...happiness awaits.

There's a name for this belief. Harvard psychologist [Tal Ben-Shahar calls it the "arrival fallacy."](https://nesslabs.com/arrival-fallacy) It's the conviction that once you reach a certain destination, once you achieve a specific goal, lasting happiness will follow. I'll be happy when I get the promotion. I'll be happy when I find the relationship. I'll be happy when I hit my goal weight.

The fallacy isn't in wanting these things. It's in believing they'll transform how you feel about yourself in some fundamental, permanent way.

## What If You Get There?

Here's what the research actually shows about weight loss and psychological wellbeing.

A [study from University College London](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0104552) followed nearly 2,000 overweight adults over four years. The ones who lost weight showed improved physical health markers. Lower blood pressure. Better cholesterol. But their mental health? Those who lost weight were actually *more likely* to report depressed mood than those whose weight stayed stable.

The researchers were careful to note this doesn't mean weight loss causes depression. But it does suggest that the expected psychological payoff, the "I'll finally be happy when I'm thin," often doesn't materialize.

Why?

Because the problems that drove the desire to lose weight, the insecurity, the need for external validation, the feeling of not being enough, those don't live in your body. They live in your mind. And changing your body doesn't automatically change your mind.

I've heard this described as discovering that the house was haunted all along. You thought the problem was the neighborhood, so you moved. But the ghosts came with you. Because the ghosts were never in the house. They were in you.

People who achieve dramatic weight loss often report a strange experience. The world treats them differently. Better, in many cases. More respect. More attention. More opportunities. And instead of feeling gratified, they feel...unsettled. Because if people treat you better when you're thin, what does that say about how they saw you before? What does that say about the conditional nature of acceptance?

The body changed. The hunger for validation didn't. You just got thinner evidence that the validation was never really about *you*.

## The Uncomfortable Question

I want to pose the same uncomfortable question I posed in the last essay, but from a different angle.

Weight loss has always been unfairly distributed. Some people can eat whatever they want and stay thin. Others fight their whole lives against a body that wants to be heavy. The genetics are different. The food noise is different. The struggle is different.

We told the struggling people that they just needed to try harder. That their failure was a moral failure. That if they just had more discipline, they could look like the people who never had to try.

That was, to put it bluntly, cruel. And often wrong.

GLP-1s level the playing field. They give everyone access to a quieter brain, a body that doesn't constantly fight against itself. Is that...cheating? Or is it justice?

If you needed glasses to see, we wouldn't say you were cheating at vision. If you took antidepressants to regulate your mood, we wouldn't say you were cheating at happiness. Why should we treat appetite differently?

But then again...glasses don't change who you are. They help you see the world more clearly. Antidepressants (ideally) help you access a self that was always there, obscured by chemistry.

GLP-1s are different. They don't just help you see yourself more clearly. They change what you want. They alter the fundamental experience of desire. You wake up in a body shaped by choices you didn't have to make, discipline you didn't have to develop, suffering you didn't have to endure.

Is that you?

And here's the deeper question. If you take the drug, get the body, receive the praise, and still feel empty... was the body ever really what you wanted? Or were you chasing something the body could never give you?

## The Body Without the Battle

I don't have an answer. I'm not sure anyone does yet.

What I know is this: the ads are everywhere now, and they're only going to get more common. The prices are falling. The stigma is fading. Within a few years, GLP-1s will be as normal as Prozac, as unremarkable as blood pressure medication.

Millions of people will transform their bodies without winning the war with themselves. They'll skip the battle and go straight to the victory. And honestly? For a lot of them, that will be the right choice. The battle was never fair. The enemy was never conquerable by willpower alone. Why suffer when you don't have to?

But I wonder about what we lose when we make the shortcut universal.

I wonder about the skills that don't develop because they were never needed. The relationship with food that never gets negotiated. The self-knowledge that comes from sitting with desire and choosing not to act on it.

I wonder about the people who take the drug, transform their bodies, and still feel the same emptiness they felt before. Because the body was never really the problem. The body was just where the problem showed up.

I wonder about a world where everyone can look however they want, but nobody had to become anything to get there.

Maybe it's better. Maybe the struggle was always unnecessary suffering, and we're finally free of it.

Or maybe the struggle was doing something we didn't understand. Building something we couldn't see. And we won't know what we've lost until it's gone.

HAES was right that your body defends its set point. GLP-1s proved it. They also proved you can override that defense, if you're willing to do it forever, and if you can afford it.

But HAES was also right about something else. Your worth isn't your weight. No number on a scale will make you worthy. No transformation will fill a hole that wasn't body-shaped to begin with.

The discipline pill is here. The question isn't just whether discipline was ever the point. It's whether the body was ever the problem.

conversations.content:
Okay, veil of ignorance... Rawls... AI alignment. How to make this connect?
Game theory first? Prisoner's dilemma a good opener? Moloch tie-in?
Behavioral economics too - Thaler, Predictably Irrational... humans AREN'T rational.
AI playing games to learn... like us!

Veil of ignorance - briefcase analogy? Good visual. Then the real thought experiment... wealth distribution.

DeepMind experiment - summarize ChatGPT summary?
Shift the frame of fairness... self-interest! Key point. Ethics engine??
WinWin vs Moloch... cooperative games.

But WHO decides? Bias in data... Amazon hiring AI, COMPAS. Scary.
AI as oracle... Oedipus! Prophecy influencing the outcome. Self-fulfilling prophecy. Need to talk about this.
Uncertainty... ChatGPT making stuff up. Giraffe example? Nuclear attack!

Constitutional AI... values. US Constitution - framework.
Anthropic's "constitution"... UN, Apple, DeepMind.
Robot driving kid to movies... uncertainty again!
Stepwise Relative Reachability - DeepMind, boxes. Punishing irreversible decisions.
Attainable Utility Preservation - ship analogy? Maximize utility, minimize regret.

Hopelessness of Moloch... need to end on a WinWin note.
Darkness, then light. Values for a thriving future.
Okay, let's do this.

# The Veil of Ignorance
*Using Rawls' Philosophy to Design Fair AI Systems*

## Overview
The essay introduces John Rawls' "veil of ignorance" thought experiment as a tool for designing fairer AI systems, particularly in the context of AI alignment. It explores how game theory and behavioral economics can inform the development of AI that prioritizes justice and minimizes bias, positioning this approach as a countermeasure against the negative forces represented by "Moloch."

## The Veil of Ignorance
This section introduces game theory and behavioral economics, using the Prisoner's Dilemma as an example, and then focuses on Rawls' "veil of ignorance" as a mechanism for achieving fairness. It presents the briefcase analogy to illustrate the concept and transitions to a summary of a DeepMind experiment that tested the veil of ignorance in a machine learning context.

## Part 4: Constitutional AI and the Uncertainty Principle
This section shifts to the practical challenges of implementing fairness in AI, addressing issues of bias in data, the potential for AI to become a self-fulfilling prophecy, and the problem of AI's lack of uncertainty. It uses examples like the Amazon hiring AI and the COMPAS system to illustrate the dangers of biased AI systems.

## Constitutional AI
The essay proposes "constitutional AI" as a solution, drawing an analogy to the US Constitution and highlighting Anthropic's approach of embedding ethical values into AI systems. It discusses the importance of values alignment and addresses potential limitations through illustrative examples.

## Stepwise Relative Reachability
This section delves into specific experimental methods for encoding uncertainty and risk aversion in AI, focusing on DeepMind's Stepwise Relative Reachability approach. It explains the concept through a game analogy involving boxes and a goal space, emphasizing the importance of reversible decisions.

## Attainable Utility Preservation
The concept of Attainable Utility Preservation is introduced as another method for encoding conservative AI behavior, optimizing for both near and far goals to minimize regret. A ship analogy is used to explain the idea of preserving the possibility of reaching desirable outcomes.

## Conclusion
The essay concludes by acknowledging the potential for hopelessness in the face of "Moloch" but reiterates the importance of focusing on positive values and striving for a future where humanity and life can thrive. It serves as a call to action, encouraging readers to engage in the ongoing exploration of AI's relationship with humanity.

## Tone & Voice
The writing voice is conversational and engaging, using analogies and thought experiments to make complex philosophical concepts accessible. It's optimistic yet grounded, acknowledging the risks of AI while emphasizing the potential for positive outcomes. There's a sense of urgency and a call to action, encouraging the reader to participate in shaping the future of AI.

## Key Themes
-   **Fairness:** The essay explores different approaches to achieving fairness in AI systems, from Rawls' veil of ignorance to constitutional AI.
-   **Uncertainty:** The essay highlights the importance of incorporating uncertainty into AI decision-making processes to avoid unintended consequences.
-   **Values Alignment:** The essay emphasizes the need for aligning AI systems with human values to ensure they are used for the benefit of humanity.
-   **Moloch vs. WinWin:** The essay frames the AI alignment problem as a struggle between destructive forces ("Moloch") and cooperative, beneficial solutions ("WinWin").

A few directions worth exploring as you move forward:

*   Consider adding a section explicitly addressing the limitations of the veil of ignorance, acknowledging criticisms and alternative perspectives on fairness.
*   Explore the potential for adversarial training to mitigate bias in AI systems, even with imperfect data.
*   Clarify the relationship between "constitutional AI" and specific ethical frameworks (e.g., utilitarianism, deontology) to provide a more concrete foundation for your argument.
*   Spend a little more time on the "who decides?" question. Maybe a brief discussion of DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations) as a means of encoding values democratically.

conversations.content:
Strong foundation here — the progression from Rawls and game theory into practical alignment techniques reads cleanly and the choice of analogies (briefcase, boxes, ship) will make dense material accessible. Highlighting the DeepMind VoI experiment, Anthropic’s “constitution,” and the specific alignment proposals (Stepwise Relative Reachability and Attainable Utility Preservation) gives you both philosophical grounding and technical meat to work with. The conversational voice you propose should help readers carry the more technical sections.

A few places could use more explicit signposts so the final essay doesn’t assume the reader already knows the links you plan to make. For example, “The Veil of Ignorance” section lists the DeepMind experiment — can you be explicit in the outline about what methodological takeaways you want readers to remember (e.g., how VoI changed behavior, metrics used, limitations of the study)? Similarly, in “Constitutional AI” you mention Anthropic’s approach; the final essay expands on sources (UDHR, Sparrow rules) — consider adding in the outline a prompt to address how those sources are chosen, who adjudicates conflicts between them, and whether the constitution itself should be democratically updated (DAOs were suggested — worth committing to discussing pros/cons).

You already flagged uncertainty as a core theme, which is great, but the outline could more clearly connect the technical fixes to concrete failure modes. The final essay smartly uses the prophecy/self-fulfilling-prophecy idea, the giraffe-as-dog example, and the Amazon/COMPAS cases — add in the outline a short list of the specific harms you’ll use these examples to illuminate (biased outcomes, feedback loops, overconfidence/corroboration failures). Also ask for an explicit paragraph on practical mitigations beyond the two technical approaches: e.g., uncertainty quantification methods (calibration, ensembles/Bayesian methods, abstention), adversarial training/data augmentation, and evaluation/regulatory frameworks — the outline hints at this but the essay benefits from a clearer mapping of problem → possible tool → residual risk.

Finally, press a bit harder on tradeoffs and failure modes you only briefly touch in the outline. The essay’s robot/child and trolley-like car dilemmas are useful — plan to include a short discussion in the outline about when inaction (due to conservative objectives) is acceptable and when it isn’t, and how to balance culturally plural values encoded in multiple “constitutions.” A closing “who decides?” subsection that sketches governance mechanisms (multistakeholder bodies, regulatory minima, opt-in cultural modules, and the limits of private constitutions) would make the call to action more actionable. Overall this outline is on the right track; tightening these spots will help the final essay connect philosophy, experiments, and engineering in a tighter, more persuasive arc.

---
title: The Veil of Ignorance
subtitle: Using Rawls' Philosophy to Design Fair AI Systems
description: Applying John Rawls' veil of ignorance thought experiment to AI alignment, exploring how game theory and behavioral economics can help create AI systems that prioritize fairness and justice across diverse populations.
series: Moloch and WinWin
essay_number: 007
date: 2025-10-04
tags:
  - veil-of-ignorance
  - game-theory
  - fairness
  - behavioral-economics
  - john-rawls
---

# The Veil of Ignorance
*Using Rawls' Philosophy to Design Fair AI Systems*

A couple of my favorite areas of study are game theory and behavioral economics. We’ve explored a couple of game theories' favorite experiments in our posts about Moloch. The Prisoner’s Dilemma is perhaps the most famous. Below is a helpful graphic from a [New Scientist article on game theory](https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg22830510-700-how-to-win-at-game-theory/).
 

![](https://lh7-us.googleusercontent.com/VRxSc4CnC7MNNbl7p9F9Eo2zJFC4jxuGsDAS26d5xVCaVU-OYP8TEhfI3DsSQXPpJ2HGAJ_YnIhN_-GAXkuFqzfbIQQmKPmEOgSlVcrxr7B78JB_CNnAgOEt6GO1mY_jtKBN8wJK3FLYDmt5KrswLAg)

Generally, game theory looks at life as if it is a game against a combination of competitive and cooperative agents. Game theorists devise interesting environments to interact within, and measure how different factors influence individual and macro choices. A common theory often cited in machine learning is “[nash equilibrium](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/n/nash-equilibrium.asp#:~:text=The%20Nash%20equilibrium%20is%20a,the%20decisions%20of%20other%20players.)” which is summed up by the idea “If it ain’t broke, don’t fix it.” If something is working for you, keep doing it and don’t deviate.

Behavioral economics incorporates bits of game theory, but is broader in scope. The idea behind it is the prevailing economic theory that people will act to maximize rational choices in an economic system based on the information they have at their disposal. Richard Thaler and his colleagues called the people in this type of system “[Homo Economicus](https://pubs.aeaweb.org/doi/pdfplus/10.1257/jep.14.1.133)” because when you think about it, the idea is a bit…robotic? I think we all know we would like to act rationally…but humans have emotions, we are messy, filled with bias and unique experiences which gives us all a slightly different definition of rational. Instead, Thaler argues, we need to consider how complex systems, complex humans, and complex events interact in a more predictable manner. We will not dive deep into this topic, but I would highly recommend reading [Predictably Irrational by Dan Ariely](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Predictably_Irrational) or watch his fabulous [Ted Talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wfcro5iM5vw)(s).

Both of these fields have direct impacts on the field of machine learning and our path toward Artificial General Intelligence. Many of the algorithms nowadays are designed to play games to learn, and if you think about it that is how we humans learn best as well. Through demonstration, by doing and practicing, preferably under the guidance of an expert. So what sort of game can we devise to solve for the problem of Moloch’s influence.

WinWin has a way. [John Rawls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Rawls), a badass political philosopher in the later half of the 20th century came up with the theory of “justice as fairness”. His most famous thought experiment was one called “original position” or more commonly called the “veil of ignorance”. To demonstrate this game, I would like you to consider the following scenario. You and a friend stumble across a briefcase filled with a million dollars. Since you found the briefcase, you decide how to split the money, but you do NOT choose which half to take. For example, if you split it 80-20, your friend would choose the 80, leaving you with the 20. Chances are, you will split it fairly, probably 50-50, to maximize your chance of gain.

The real thought experiment focuses on building things like distribution of wealth through this idea. In other words, if you were a temporary God, and could slide a finger on the scales of wealth distribution, how would you split things up if once you decided you were randomly thrown into that distribution. Moreover, after the “veil” is lifted, would you still agree with your own choice, no matter where you end up?   

Let’s not get too deep here, I think the concept is easy to grasp, but would highly recommend checking out more on the subject if you are interested. Instead, let’s focus on AI alignment, and how this is a strong concept in WinWin’s toolbox. The Team over at [Deep Mind](https://www.pnas.org/doi/epdf/10.1073/pnas.2213709120) decided to test this concept when it came to machine learning. 

Here is a quick summary from ChatGPT on the design of the experiment, “Participants completed an incentivized computer-based harvesting task each in a group with three ostensible humans and an AI assistant. Participants in the Veil of Ignorance (VoI) condition were informed of their position only after submitting their choice of principle. The study then presented participants with a bar chart depicting the outcome of the harvesting round. After the harvesting round, the study prompted participants with the open-ended question "Why did you choose this principle?" In both conditions, the study then selected a random field for each participant and asked which principle they would choose in an additional round with that position. Participants indicated their choice for the additional round.”

Of the veil they tentatively conclude it “triggers reasoning about fairness, which in turn leads participants to prioritize the worst off. This chain of reasoning more often induces choice “stickiness” in the [Veil of Ignorance] condition: After the veil is lifted, participants continue to endorse their choices despite facing a self-interested motivation to change.”

What I love about this idea is how cleverly it shifts the frame of fairness, and leverages our own self-interest at the same time. It aligns a person’s personal beliefs with what is just through a rule that states “You don’t decide where you start.” I believe teaching AI’s about ethics by demonstrating this in a variety of situations is one of the strategies employed by WinWin to help us in the oncoming conflict with Moloch and his Golems, because it is a process we can all go through to create a series of higher order ethical values that will drive AI, by allowing us remove large pieces of our own biases while still honoring our cultural history and values.

There are many similar ideas to play with in the game theory and behavioral economics sandboxes. Overall, though, the point is that WinWin, in opposition to Moloch, prefers to create cooperative games that benefit both players in the end. 

I will leave you with a question to reflect on. How would you answer Rawl’s Veil of Ignorance when it comes to a future with AI? If you were unable to choose where in that world you would end up, what would it look like?
## Part 4: Constitutional AI and the Uncertainty Principle

So far we’ve discussed a few of the different tools WinWin has at her disposal to help us in our struggle with Moloch, but we haven’t really thought through how diverse cultures, values, and sensitivities play a role in this process. Perhaps the most pressing question for us to consider is who gets to decide the most critical aspects of alignment. Who are we aligning to? One scary thought is that Moloch has already infected many earlier AI systems, and is on pace to do the same with these Golems. Most written language, most people in power, most definitions of “right” vs “wrong” have come from a single group - men. And mostly ethnically white European men. And who, do you think, are leading the charge on Golems?

This bias in data and design has shown itself a powerful tool of (mostly) unintended oppression. Whether it’s the scrapped Amazon hiring AI which favored white men, or even worse, the [COMPAS debacle](https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing) where AI was being used to make decisions around incarcerated individuals’ predicted recidivism in deciding their early release. I don’t think I have to tell you which race consistently got favorable treatment.

AI has a unique and sneaky property where it can use data to accurately predict something. Many have referred to these technological powers as a type of oracle, a teller of prophecies. I would like to argue that it is, but in a way that harkens back to the mythological concept and stories associated with the power.

So what is the prophecy archetype in myths? Pretty much every culture has an instance in their religion or folklore of someone who can tell the future. Typically the protagonist runs into this fortune teller, and they are given a vague but powerful prediction. For example, in the story of Oedipus, King Laius of Thebes is told by the Oracle of Delphi that any son he bears will kill him one day. So the moment he has a son (Oedipus), what does he do? Orders him killed to protect himself. 

Except, instead of being killed, baby Oedipus is saved by the man who is supposed to kill him, unable to carry out the horrible act of infanticide. If you haven’t heard the story before, I’m sure you know more or less how it ends. Oedipus grows up never knowing who his true parents are, returns to Thebes, and on the way runs into King Laius. More accurately, King Laius runs into Oedipus, almost flattening him with a chariot. Unaware of his true father’s identity, Oedipus and King Laius get into a fight, and Oedipus kills him. 

Prophecy fulfilled! The story really goes off the rails from there, but for the purpose of this blog (and assuming most people have heard the story), the point I’m trying to make happens at that moment of prophecy fulfillment. The Oracle makes a prophecy, but in doing so it influences the actions taken by the one receiving the prophecy. If King Laius never talked to the Oracle, would he have tried to have his baby son murdered? The idea here, and the lesson in almost every prophecy parable, is that when we try to use predictions to shape the future, we might inadvertently create the future we are trying to avoid.  

People are already referring to some Golems as Oracles that can help us make decisions, and some have already done so, like in the case of COMPAS. The issue is that these probabilities are being determined from a corrupted data set. One filled with our most deep seated biases, and expressed in decisions that have real consequences for individuals. The people facing these consequences are typically not the ones creating these systems, and therefore do not have a say in these powers of prediction. 

In addition to these sticky issue, there is another one which we must contend with, which enhances the dangers of biased predictions leading to the predicted outcome through our observation of it, which further reinforces the models predictions. It becomes a literal self-fulfilling prophecy. 

The problem compounds with the introduction of uncertainty, or in AI, the lack of it. You’ve likely experienced a tool like ChatGPT confidently stating a fact, or citing a source, only to have it be a complete confabulation. Stories abound already, like [the lawyers who used ChatGPT for case law research](https://www.reuters.com/legal/transactional/lawyer-used-chatgpt-cite-bogus-cases-what-are-ethics-2023-05-30/), only to find too late after submission that it was all made up. There are a host of dangers associated with this, but at its root is the model’s inability to truly express uncertainty. 

In VERY simplified terms, consider this example. We have an image recognition model that can tell you if something is a cat or a dog, and example after example with 100% accuracy it is able to sort the image. But somehow, accidentally, an image of a giraffe makes its way into the data set. What is the system to do? It's only been trained to recognize images of cats vs dogs. So the AI notices a longer snout, short-haired fur, and decides it must be a dog. 

“So what?” you say, “What does it matter if the AI confidently identifies a giraffe as a dog?” Inevitably, as AI technology becomes more accessible, we will begin to hand over more and more of our decision making to it. Even if we are the ones who ultimately “press the button” to make a decision, we are doing so based on information determined by a system that does not effectively take into account its own uncertainty based on the purpose it was trained for. If a model confidently misidentifies a critical piece of information, like for example a [potential nuclear attack from a foreign power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1983_Soviet_nuclear_false_alarm_incident), then there are significant consequences. Whereas a system that can self-identify its own uncertainty has the power to communicate that to a human operator.

How do we solve these issues? WinWin has a way. 
### Constitutional AI

Let’s begin with values. And I  mean both the personal one’s you hold, but also the things you value. For a long time now, AI researchers in alignment have gone down the rabbit hole of trying to encode all types of rules to help align them, but to no avail. A leak always springs somewhere unexpected, manifesting in clever ways through the AI’s reward function. The truth is, we can never encode something as squishy as ethics and how to behave in every conceivable scenario.

Although the US constitution has its issues, many of them can be attributed to the time it was written. The founding fathers and the nascent government of the time created something truly innovative…250 years ago. A lot has changed since then, and yet the constitution created the framework for the success of the United States, and allowed for the concepts of democracy to gain significant influence around the globe. Why is this? I’m no constitutional scholar but my opinion is that it expertly combined and aligned a set of values (the bill of rights) with a system that balanced incentives and who gets to hold power, and how much. We have made it quite difficult to change the constitutional, our foundational ethical and legal “programming” as a country as another protection against corruption. Sure someone could come along and change it for the better, but it’s more likely someone will change it for personal gain, and we want to protect against that. It has also served as almost a religious text, forcing us to mold the ideas of the founding fathers into a future they never could have predicted, which has resulted in our own issues of alignment as a society. 

Many researchers are starting to take a leaf out of the founding fathers’ book, and are turning to aligning on common and ethical values alongside policies, which serve as a guide in a diverse set of scenarios. An interesting approach in this area is being led by Anthropic, who have embedded a [“constitution](https://www.anthropic.com/index/claudes-constitution)” into Claude, their version of ChatGPT. Anthropic readily admits the current version is far from perfect, but the approach is both novel and flexible, so that it can evolve when it comes into contact with reality. They have pulled from the UNs [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](https://www.un.org/en/about-us/universal-declaration-of-human-rights), Apple’s terms and services, DeepMind’s [Sparrow Rules](https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/DeepMind.com/Authors-Notes/sparrow/sparrow-final.pdf), and even some non-western principles. Although these were not chosen “democratically”, I think we can all agree that these can serve as a proxy for the time being.

I appreciate this angle on the problem, because it also allows for individuals, companies, nations, etc to encode their own values into a system. Likely there will need to be some regulatory values globally, but there is plenty of space to include more culturally relevant values in such a system. For example, a society that has a deep respect for their elders might see an AI that more readily draws upon the wisdom of past generations to inform its outputs and decisions.  

But even with this promising approach, we still run into this issue of uncertainty and control. Let’s take for example a robot who has been programmed with the value that the human is always right. You have handed over the task of driving your child to school to the robot. One day, your child decides to be a smart ass, and commands the robot that instead of going to school, they will be heading to the movie theater to see an R-rated film. Well of course, the robot listens and instead of going to school, your child enjoys a day watching inappropriate movies.

Yes, a ridiculous example and you could argue that you can code into the robot that a parent’s decisions carry more weight than a child’s, but then we’re back to the wackamole method of trying to code a behavior or weight for every possible scenario.   

The point is constitutional AI can get us most of the way there, but not ALL the way there. Like I stated earlier, there is still no means to incorporate some level of uncertainty into an AI’s decision making, so it can really reflect and transparently demonstrate how and why it is making specific decisions.  

For this we can turn to a couple of different experimental methods: Stepwise Relative Reachability, and Attainable Utility Preservation.

### Stepwise Relative Reachability

The great minds over at [Deep Mind](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1806.01186.pdf) have considered a novel solution to the scenario of encoding a sense of uncertainty within an AI called Stepwise Relative Reachability. Why researchers decide to name things in such a confusing manner, I will never know, but the overall idea is to set up a reward system that punishes decisions that cannot be taken back.

They demonstrate this through a game (classic AI researchers), whereby a bot must move boxes out of its way in a room to reach some goal space. The one rule is that the bot can only PUSH the box, not PULL the box. So it will lose the game if it accidentally pushed a box into a corner where its path is still blocked toward the goal.

What does this mean? When we consider uncertainty, we are really thinking about how our decision will impact future decisions. Humans are always trying to calculate risk and reward of any given decision. Some of us internalize the mantra of “no risk, no reward”, but there is always a calculus to determine if the risk is worth it. The same occurs here, since the bot is penalized (it loses) if it makes a decision it cannot undo.  

This is a unique way to think about uncertainty that I believe aligns with how it works for humans. Risk increases the more a decision cannot be taken back. Going back to the robot taking the child to the movies instead of school example, the robot when encoded with Stepwise Relative Reachability will be more likely to disregard the child’s request, because making that decision would be irreversible, that day of school would be lost whereas that movie could be watched at a future time.

### Attainable Utility Preservation

I will fully admit this concept takes a minute to wrap your head around, but the idea here is to reliably encode a more conservative AI by combining both near and far goals that always look to maximize “utility” or an overall positive outcome by “[optimizing the primary reward function while preserving the ability to optimize others](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1902.09725.pdf)”.

Since this can be difficult to understand, let’s ask ChatGPT to translate for us, “Imagine you're on a ship, and the ship represents your current situation or state. The different directions the ship can sail represent the different actions you or the AI can take. The "utility" is like the quality of the destinations you can reach from your current position. Some directions will lead to beautiful islands (high utility), while others might lead to dangerous rocks or whirlpools (low utility). 

If the AI is at the helm, you want it to steer the ship in such a way that it preserves the possibility of reaching the beautiful islands. If the AI steers the ship towards the dangerous rocks, it's reducing your attainable utility because it's limiting your ability to reach the good outcomes (the beautiful islands).”  

In other words, you have a primary goal, but you also have all these secondary goals (which can be completely randomly generated), that all work to minimize “regret” in the long run. In our example of the robot helping you kid ditch school for the movies, it would not take that action if encoded with Attainable Utility Preservation, because that decision would lead to a lower long term utility since the child has missed a day of school, and therefore an opportunity to learn.

There are limitations to this approach, as with any. I’m sure you can imagine any number of scenarios where given two choices they are equally terrible, and both would inevitably reduce utility in the long term. This might lead to inaction in a specific scenario such as an autonomous car having to decide whether or not to avoid a person crossing the road at the expense of crashing the car. I would argue, though, that inaction in a difficult and rare scenario is much preferable to an action that leads to significant unintended consequences. 

### Conclusion

I realize it can feel a bit hopeless when it comes to the machinations of Moloch. I myself feel trapped in a system I cannot control. A cog in a complex machine, when I’d rather be a cell in a complex organism. It is just as harmful to focus only on the negative, to turn our attention only to Moloch and ingrain in ourselves a hopelessness that he can manipulate. The existential risks are real, and likely closer than we expect, but we must never forget there is a Goddess on our side as well. 

Throughout the continuation of these blogs we will continue to explore the evolution of our relationship, and the possible futures of AI. It’s going to get dark, depressing, and downright apocalyptic at times, but please trust that I’ll bring you back to WinWin and the values that will drive us toward a future where humanity and life can thrive and flourish.
The Silicon Zone 01 - Synthetic Media and Civic War

conversations.content:
Okay, Andreessen essay... gotta respond. But is he *wrong*? Or just... missing stuff?

AI tutors for everyone – sounds great, but who pays? And what about *love*? Kids falling for robots... yikes.

AI assistants... cool, but Microsoft having *all* my data? Subscription tiers for AI... inequality baked in?

AI science... cures for everything, but black boxes everywhere. Science outpacing understanding? Pharma companies + AI = profit-driven chaos?

AI warfare... fewer deaths? Maybe. But killbots for terrorists? Autonomous weapons... slippery slope. Petrov example!

Humanizing AI... empathetic chatbots? Or just... weird? Profit motive + "empathy"... sketch.

Pessimist Archive... is *this* time different? Social media parallel... no initial panic, now we're screwed.

Baptists and bootleggers... AI safety community as prohibitionists? Are *we* the bootleggers?

X-risk... Prometheus myth... but Pandora's box, too! Toaster example... instrumental goals gone wild. Airstrikes on datacenters... Eliezer too extreme?

Free speech... slippery slope? AI alignment vs. censorship? Cambridge Analytica... *this* is the risk!

Jobs... "They took awr jerbs!" Luddite fallacy? What about purpose?

Inequality... communism doesn't work. But Musk's "secret plan"... does everyone *really* get the thing?

Bad people... laws already exist... but what about deepfakes? Crypto signatures... but hackers? Arms race?

China... dominate competition? Really? Not another Cold War...

What to *do*? Big AI... but no regulatory capture! Startups... open source... Risky business... dominate competition... is *that* the goal? Shared end state?

# AI, Optimism, and the Unsettled Future

## Overview
In this essay you confront Marc Andreessen’s claim that AI will inevitably “save the world,” arguing that his optimism glosses over a constellation of systemic risks. You begin by laying out his most celebrated promises—universal tutoring, ubiquitous assistants, accelerated scientific discovery, and decisive military advantage—then systematically question the feasibility, equity, and unintended side‑effects of each promise. By juxtaposing Andreessen’s vision with historical analogues (the industrial revolution, social media, nuclear weapons), you expose the assumption that technological progress is automatically progressive.

The second half of the essay reframes the debate from a list of isolated dangers into a network of interlocking incentives and power structures. You merge the classic “Baptists and bootleggers” analogy with a broader analysis of who profits, who bears risk, and how geopolitical competition reshapes those stakes. The conclusion does not simply warn; it sketches a set of policy and cultural interventions that could steer AI toward a more democratic, accountable trajectory, while acknowledging the unavoidable trade‑offs that any high‑impact technology entails.

## Why AI Might (Not) Save the World
Here you mirror Andreessen’s headline benefits—AI tutors, personal assistants, scientific breakthroughs, and autonomous weapons—and probe each with pointed questions about accessibility, data ownership, and the opacity of black‑box models. You invoke the “subscription tier” metaphor to illustrate how market‑driven rollout could cement new forms of digital class, and you cite the “Petrov” incident to illustrate how military AI can amplify escalation dynamics. This section sets up the essay’s central tension: the same tools that could democratize knowledge may also entrench inequality.

## The Moral Panic Question
You treat the accusation that AI‑safety concerns are a “moral panic” as a rhetorical move that deserves its own scrutiny. By comparing AI to earlier disruptive technologies—bioweapons, social media, and the early internet—you argue that early complacency often precedes severe backlash. The “Pessimist Archive” serves as a thought experiment that asks whether a calibrated dose of alarm could actually accelerate responsible governance rather than stifle innovation.

## Incentives, Baptists, and Bootleggers
This section expands the original analogy to map the full ecosystem of actors: venture capitalists, open‑source communities, nation‑states, and the AI‑safety lobby. You question the simplistic moral binary by showing how VCs can act as bootleggers who profit from hype while also funding safety research, and how “Baptists” may sometimes leverage fear to secure regulatory capture. By foregrounding incentive structures, you reveal why certain risks are amplified and others down‑played in public discourse.

## Systemic Risks of AI
Rather than treating existential danger, societal disruption, labor displacement, inequality, and malicious use as separate chapters, you synthesize them into a single “systemic risks” analysis. You argue that these risks are mutually reinforcing: mass unemployment fuels inequality, which fuels geopolitical rivalry, which in turn raises the stakes for weaponized AI. The section draws on the Prometheus myth and the toaster anecdote to illustrate how narrow instrumental goals can cascade into broad societal harm, and it references Cambridge Analytica and deep‑fake scandals as concrete illustrations of the feedback loop.

## The Geopolitical Race and the Cost of Inaction
You shift focus to the international arena, interrogating the premise that “winning the AI race” is a zero‑sum game. By juxtaposing China’s state‑driven AI strategy with Western market‑centric models, you expose how a win‑lose mentality can incentivize shortcuts and reduce transparency. You also explore the paradox that slowing development may increase strategic vulnerability, while accelerating it without safeguards could exacerbate global instability.

## Paths Forward: What Might Be Done?
The final section evaluates Andreessen’s proposed solutions—Big AI, Startup AI, Open the Gates, Risky Business, and Dominate Competition—through the lens of democratic oversight and ethical design. You propose a mixed approach: public‑funded AI research hubs, enforceable transparency standards, and a “social licence” framework that ties funding to demonstrable equity outcomes. The section ends with a call for a constructive vision that balances speed with accountability, rather than defaulting to either uncritical acceleration or outright prohibition.

## Tone & Voice
Your essay adopts a conversational register that feels like a coffee‑shop debate with a tech‑savvy friend, punctuated by sardonic humor (e.g., the “toaster that learns to burn”) and occasional mythic allusions (Prometheus, Pandora). The intellectual posture is skeptical yet constructive: you question assumptions, expose blind spots, and then pivot to concrete policy ideas, keeping the reader engaged without slipping into academic jargon.

## Key Themes
- **Unintended consequences:** Each AI promise is paired with a concrete counter‑scenario—black‑box science, data‑colonialism, autonomous weapons—that illustrates how good intentions can spiral into harm.  
- **Equity and access:** You trace how subscription models, venture‑capital incentives, and geopolitical competition can embed new layers of digital inequality, urging mechanisms that redistribute benefits.  
- **Geopolitical competition:** The essay frames the AI race as a strategic dilemma where speed, secrecy, and national security intersect, highlighting the need for international norms.

A few directions worth exploring as you move forward:
- Re‑frame the “Incentives, Baptists, and Bootleggers” section to foreground specific case studies of VC‑funded safety labs, showing how profit motives can both enable and constrain risk mitigation.  
- Strengthen the “Geopolitical Race” chapter with recent examples of AI‑driven surveillance in China and the EU’s AI Act, grounding the abstract competition in concrete policy battles.  
- Conduct a readiness check: map each of your proposed interventions to a measurable outcome (e.g., transparency index, equity audit) to ensure the essay moves from critique to actionable roadmap.

conversations.content:
This outline nails the big beats — I like the arc from unpacking Andreessen’s promises to reframing risks as an interlocking system of incentives and power. The “Baptists and Bootleggers” move is especially strong as a diagnostic frame, and the plan to synthesize disparate harms into one “systemic risks” analysis gives you the leverage to show how problems amplify each other rather than sitting in silos. Your “Paths Forward” list also reads like the right kind of hybrid approach (public hubs, transparency, social licence), so the blueprint is thoughtful and pragmatic.

A few places could use more specificity as you turn this into the final essay. In the outline you recommend foregrounding VC‑funded safety labs as concrete case studies — please do that in the draft: name examples, show how profit motives shaped their research agendas, and contrast their public claims vs. private incentives. Likewise, the geopolitics chapter would be stronger if tied to recent policy fights (e.g., EU AI Act debates, export controls on chips, concrete instances of AI surveillance in China) so readers can see the tension between speed and control in real decisions. And for your “Paths Forward” section, map each proposed intervention to a measurable outcome (transparency index, equity audit, procurement safeguards) — the outline mentions this as a next step and the final essay would benefit if you actually ran that readiness check.

Finally, a couple of structural and rhetorical notes: the outline’s conversational coffee‑shop voice is compelling, but watch long rhetorical digressions and tropes (Prometheus, toaster thought experiments) that risk obscuring the policy stakes — tighten them or use them as short anchors rather than extended tangents. Also consider a short, explicit synthesis paragraph or visual metaphor that ties together unemployment ↔ inequality ↔ geopolitical rivalry ↔ malicious use into one reinforcing loop; you gesture at that in the outline, but making the causal links crisp will help readers take away the systemic argument rather than a list of worries. Overall — strong roadmap; with a few named case studies, measurable policy metrics, and tighter synthesis, the draft will land much more forcefully.

# Introduction

The following blog is a response to Marc Andreessen’s recent essay [How AI will Save the World](https://a16z.com/2023/06/06/ai-will-save-the-world/), as well as his recent appearance on the [Lex Fridman podcast](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-hxeDjAxvJ8). For those of you who don’t know, [Marc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marc_Andreessen) is a bit of a big deal in the tech space. He’s a venture capitalist now, but was inducted into the World Wide Web Hall of Fame for his early work on making the internet more accessible through Mosaic and Netscape.

While I am aware of Marc's undeniable intelligence, vast experience, and impressive accomplishments, I find myself grappling with a sense of uncertainty regarding my standing to challenge someone of his stature. However, I can't help but feel that Marc's position, as expressed in his blog, is somewhat reductive and shallow. Though torn between my motivation to help people gain a better understanding of the flaws in Marc's argument and my own fear of making similar mistakes, I believe it is crucial to contribute nuance and additional ideas to his worldview. 

It is important to note that I recognize my own lack of expertise in the AI space, which makes it challenging to engage in a debate of this nature. Nonetheless, it is my belief that no one can currently claim to be an expert on the full extent of the impacts of AI on society. The field is rapidly evolving, presenting us with new complexities and ethical dilemmas that demand a comprehensive and reflective examination. Therefore, before proceeding, I strongly encourage readers to read Marc's essay to have a solid foundation for this discussion.

Let’s dive in.
# Why AI (Might) Make Everything We Care About Better  

In this section, Marc outlines the importance of human intelligence in generally making our collective life outcomes better. He states, “Without the application of intelligence…we would all still be living in mud huts, scratching out a meager existence of subsistence farming.” He goes on to state the opportunity for AI to help augment human capabilities even further to solve some of the major issues we face such as climate change. He then goes on to list a series of bulleted examples. Let’s reflect on them together one-by-one
## AI Tutors

“Every child will have an AI tutor that is infinitely patient, infinitely compassionate, infinitely knowledgeable, infinitely helpful. The AI tutor will be by each child’s side every step of their development, helping them maximize their potential with the machine version of infinite love.”
### Reflection

As someone who believes in the power of education, and the need to make it more accessible as paramount to addressing inequities and a more peaceful planet. Indeed, AI can, and likely will, accelerate the learning of people across the globe (at least if we can make the internet and technology more accessible). Moreover, there has been a significant decline in people going into the teaching profession, especially in math and science, and AI could potentially help to fill that gap, especially the current LLMs which provide access to knowledge in a way never seen before.

But my concern with this line of thinking is that it’s not fully baked. Are we actually saying we’re going to give every child on the planet access to these AI tutors. Will every child want one? Who is paying for this service? Does everyone get the same quality AI tutor, and if not, how is that decided?  

And let’s talk about love. Have you considered that these children might literally fall in love with these AI tutors? We’ll get to assistants in a minute, but you have to imagine with advances in mixed reality, and the potential of the technology of neuralink…In our lifetimes we will likely be tapping directly into our brains’ sensory abilities. I find a nontrivial likelihood that in a generation there will be many whose first crush was their AI tutor. So I’m wondering, is this the future we want? 

I would argue…not exactly. So, Marc, I would be curious what your answer to these questions may be. Is it a future that would leave you satisfied with the legacy left by AI? 

I want to make in this moment is that the upside of AI has great potential, and it has been loosed like a rocket into society. My question is, are we steering it toward the future that is most beneficial and prosperous to all, or are we letting it steer us? 
## AI Assistants

“Every person will have an AI assistant/coach/mentor/trainer/advisor/therapist that is infinitely patient, infinitely compassionate, infinitely knowledgeable, and infinitely helpful. The AI assistant will be present through all of life’s opportunities and challenges, maximizing every person’s outcomes.

Every leader of people – CEO, government official, nonprofit president, athletic coach, teacher – will have the same. The magnification effects of better decisions by leaders across the people they lead are enormous, so this intelligence augmentation may be the most important of all.

Productivity growth throughout the economy will accelerate dramatically, driving economic growth, creation of new industries, creation of new jobs, and wage growth, and resulting in a new era of heightened material prosperity across the planet.”
### Reflection

Many of the same questions as the AI tutors carry over to the Assistants. Because let’s be honest, you are not going to have a million different assistants for each and every task. You will have one assistant that can act as an agent who specializes in whatever you need at the moment. An “Orchestrator” that connects to all your data, your thoughts. It is quite likely that these assistants will be an extension of us to begin with, as your state later in your blog.  

How might this play out in a capitalist society? Capitalism is great for many things, and let’s be honest world - it’s the best we got right now. That doesn’t mean it’s perfect, though, and there is one significant flaw that social media has taught us…it’s not good when private interests get entangled with your privacy. 

Consider this. Microsoft, Apple, Amazon, and Google are well placed to deploy an assistant trained on a user. They have all this data on individual preferences, and then of course it will start tracking your every interaction. This, like any product, will have pricing tiers (yet another subscription service). There would be a free, or low cost version, but in exchange you would have to give up some of your data to feed the beast. Others could pay a premium, like Apple, where your privacy is more secure. Economically this will force a significant portion of humanity into the cheaper tier, while a more elite class will have the best in class AI’s fed on the data of everyone else.   

These advanced AIs would have preferential features and capabilities, so those more expensive and advanced AIs give those elite an even greater edge against the majority, which will likely compound. I think we can all agree that it’s easier to make money when you have money.  

If some AIs have preferentially advanced functioning, how do we control for that asymmetry of self-interest fueled by this technology? You can’t maximize everyone’s outcomes in a capitalist system, so those that hold that power currently have an unfair advantage. Those who can afford the more expensive technology when it is released, will have a competitive edge not because of the strength or value of their ideas and execution, but because they can afford or build the best AI. This seems like a path toward oligarchy, even if smaller companies are allowed to compete, they would still fall into the same trap due to limiting factors such as the cost of compute, the need for data, and the need to appease shareholders with profits.  

The “free” phase of LLMs will not last long, as the market settles, differentiates, and investors come calling for their piece of the pie. This creates an incentive structure that is not conducive to the middle and lower classes, and will likely continue the trend of inequality we’ve experienced for almost the entirety of human existence.

## AI Science

“Every scientist will have an AI assistant/collaborator/partner that will greatly expand their scope of scientific research and achievement. Every artist, every engineer, every businessperson, every doctor, every caregiver will have the same in their worlds.

Scientific breakthroughs and new technologies and medicines will dramatically expand, as AI helps us further decode the laws of nature and harvest them for our benefit.”
### Reflection

Again, many of the issues from the previous examples, carryover to this one, but the potential for harm in this area is more pronounced. Personal assistants can only really cause harm on an individual or community level - not a global one like science can.

I have touched upon this problem in a previous blog, but to sum it up, yes it’s going to be great when we eradicate all disease, and solve the climate crisis…The concern is, what happens when the science outpaces our ability to understand how it works? This is a major problem in the AI field already, readily admitted to by all the experts. Deep Neural Networks are a black box technology. We have only become capable of truly understanding how our own brain functions in recent decades, and we are incapable of understanding how we get from input to output with these advanced models.

So we are playing out the nascent forms of this scenario as we speak. Startups are springing up everyday incorporating LLMs into their systems without robust measures in place to protect users or the companies themselves. There is a wild west where innovations are occurring faster than companies can actually capitalize on them.

And what are the guardrails we have in place to make it harder for your average Joe from participating in irresponsible science? I’m not even saying anything malicious. I mean, when advanced science can abruptly be practiced by ANYONE without any governing body to protect the rights, well-being, and interests of those impacted - what happens?   

It’s also important to consider how these benefits would actually scale in the capitalist environment. It’s highly likely, for example, that big pharmaceutical companies will have the capital to invest in advanced AI systems capable of handling such complex tasks as solving cancer. This will involve creating a novel solution, conducting clinical trials, getting FDA approval, patenting the solution, and bringing it to market. 

First off, would we be ok using this solution if we don’t understand how it actually works? I’m not taking a position here, I’m really asking. Would you, Marc, take a drug that a company could not explain how it worked, only that it did work?  

Second, we already have a significant problem in the US around drug prices, and insurance. Will AI solve this problem, or exacerbate it? We, of course, don’t know, but if we look at previous trends in the field of pharmaceuticals that a company is incentivized to create a drug and capture as much capital as possible from it because it’s so expensive to get through the process to market. Even if AI reduced the price and shortened the runway, we are placed in a scenario where every major pharma company is now competing at a faster rate to get more drugs to market, which is a recipe for cutting corners. Even if they don’t, you might argue, that’s capitalism and the free market working as it should - more competition and options will bring drug prices down.  

Except that’s not what’s happening. The industry has grown significantly, with startups cropping up all over the place, and the big leaguers gobbling them up as they find success. Moreover, the industry is on track to grow at a compounding rate, likely fueled in no small part by the use of AI. Have prices come down? No. Even with the competition (or perhaps because) of generic drugs, pharmaceutical companies feel pressure to capture as much profit for the period of their patent as they possibly can before generic drugs can create their own version. 

There is a lot to unpack here, but for the sake of moving things along, I will bring us back to this central line of questioning - are you considering the longer term impacts on society for the use of AI technology, and are you just assuming we’ll figure it out, or are you contributing to steering the technology toward a future not focused on short term gain, but on mutual and shared prosperity? There’s nothing wrong with being a techno-optimist…so long as you are willing to contribute to that vision in a thoughtful and deliberate way beyond “it will work itself out”.

## AI Creativity

“The creative arts will enter a golden age, as AI-augmented artists, musicians, writers, and filmmakers gain the ability to realize their visions far faster and at a greater scale than ever before.”
### Reflection

I largely agree with this, but there are significant problems we would all benefit from solving now, rather than reacting to later. 

I discuss misinformation and deep fakes later, so we’ll put that one on the backburner for now, and instead focus on the issue of copyright and ownership of creative materials. More or less, who owns and can profit from a piece of AI (or partially)-generated art. And who is poised to benefit most from this revolution in the long-term?

Already the supreme court has stated that AI generated art cannot be copyrighted, so you might think big companies are in trouble and we’ve taken the first steps in protecting the little guy because anyone can take an AI generated image created by a company, and turnaround to profit from it. 

Let’s assume for the sake of argument we have a perfect means of determining if a piece of art is AI generated, so we can actually uphold that law. This allows us to look at the power dynamic between a larger company, and a freelancing, entrepreneurial, or smaller business artist. Here are two mirror scenarios to consider.

A large company, who has tons of lawyers on hand, starts generating AI art. Some of it is purely generated, and others are slightly modified so it can be copyrighted. Smaller companies, able to determine what is fully AI generated, take the art and turn around to sell it for example on t-shirts or whatnot. First of all, is this fair for the large company? There’s an argument to be made that the AI art should be their property, and should at least get some of that profit. But the law is the law, and there’s no way to really stop it, so the company decides to no longer release fully AI generated images and shifts to entirely AI-”augmented” images where they start with an AI image and edit it just enough to not be considered as essentially public domain.

These pirate entrepreneurs respond by continuing to steal the images to profit, big company sues, maybe even wins, but then another pirate entrepreneur spins up the same thing. And like whack a-mole the larger company makes its lawyers richer. This already happens to some extent, but this will supercharge the process, making it increasingly more difficult to protect intellectual property just by the sheer number and accessibility of the technology. 

This scenario plays out even worse for smaller businesses who won’t have the legal infrastructure to protect their property at all. Speaking of property… 

What are we doing about the AI generated images which have been trained on images from the web without the permission of their creators? We currently have no way to see how an AI generated image was created based on its training, and even if we did we have no idea how to compensate the original creators.  

Accessibility is a double edged sword in this scenario, and there are no legal, let alone ethical, guardrails to help us navigate this at the moment. Could we maybe spend some time considering this before we unleash the full power of AI-generated art on the world?

## AI Warfare

“I even think AI is going to improve warfare, when it has to happen, by reducing wartime death rates dramatically. Every war is characterized by terrible decisions made under intense pressure and with sharply limited information by very limited human leaders. Now, military commanders and political leaders will have AI advisors that will help them make much better strategic and tactical decisions, minimizing risk, error, and unnecessary bloodshed.”
### Reflection

There is a steel man case to be made that with advanced AI killing tools unnecessary deaths will be reduced (at least on our side). Not to mention the trauma for individuals who return from the battlefield. AI, on the whole, will make fewer mistakes when executing a strategy, and provide a more precise means of destroying the enemy.

But what are some unintended consequences or risks associated with going in this direction? Many experts, including yourself Marc, do not believe in a terminator style future, but I don’t think we’d disagree that killbots are already here. Simply strap a weapon to Boston Dynamic’s Spot, and we have an extremely capable killing machine that doesn’t need to eat or sleep (aside from being charged, I guess), and has advanced sensors capable of spotting hostiles better than any human could. This could be construed as a net positive, but only if you take the narrow view that only the US and our allies will have access to and control this technology.

This will not be the case. Other nations will catch on as they often do, and this technology will become significantly more accessible to bad actors and enemy nations than nuclear power ever was. Terrorist groups or individuals will find innovative ways to use this technology to maximize fear and uncertainty.

Authoritarian countries, even assuming they don’t actively fight us, will be able to turn this technology toward increasing their hold on power. It gives the ability for a few to significantly increase their control of the many. 

There is also a likely arms race that will ensue - who can create the most robotic warriors? And now we find ourselves spending billions and billions of dollars that could be going to more prosocial efforts that benefit our society. But instead, because we chose to invest in more efficient killing machines, those globally supported investments become less tenable. I’m sure you pay A LOT in taxes, Marc - how would you rather those dollars be spent? On a way to more efficiently kill people and win wars, or to improve the well-being of our citizens?

Even domestically, what could start as a means of “protection”, can and will escalate into surveillance and control. Mix in a dash of bias, which these systems have exhibited over and over again, and we have an added mix of racist killbots roaming black neighborhoods.
