Will you still hold this opinion after the first innocent black man is shot by a killbot? Will you argue that, well, the robot is 75% less likely to shoot an innocent black man than a police officer?

And yes, you’re probably right, these killbots will be much better at targeting the real bad guys, and not killing innocent civilians. But how will we deal with the invetiable mistakes. The oft cited example of Petrov narrowly averting a full blown nuclear war by ignoring his radar which said nukes were coming from the US is a great example of this in action. AI might on the whole be better at targeting, alerting, etc., but it will make mistakes, and when it does, who will be responsible?  

Maybe, you think, this is just the cost of doing business. That the utilitarian view of the greatest good is the mantra we’ll want to adhere to, but I’m not so sure. I think this is a short-term view. Yes, I believe at first this will be a distinct advantage that can help continue and grow US dominance on a geopolitical stage, but at what cost? If we make killing more accessible, and it is easier to resort to that decision because fewer of our lives are at stake, and you don’t physically have to pull a trigger, or mobilize human beings…do you see the potential consequence? When you reduce friction in making a decision, you make that decision easier to make, right? So we have to ask ourselves - do we want to make it easier to kill people?

And I know you’ll bring in the argument, “China and Russia are going to do it, so we must do it as well to get ahead.” And maybe they will. But until they do…do we need to make this a priority? Like I said, we can already duct tape together a version of this, and as robotics improve, it will become easier for us to create killbots, but do we need to be the ones to jumpstart the race? There are so many other ways to spend our money in ways that will benefit not just us, but potentially relations with our global opponents. 

I know I’m being naive and optimistic, but maybe…just maybe, if we declare to the world we are not going to continue down this route, and stick with what we’ve already developed, and demonstrate that by not utilizing these efficient killing tools, others might also pause. That’s how I’d like to see us lead, what about you?
## Humanizing AI

“And this isn’t just about intelligence! Perhaps the most underestimated quality of AI is how humanizing it can be. AI art gives people who otherwise lack technical skills the freedom to [create and share their artistic ideas](https://www.forbes.com/sites/tjmccue/2023/01/23/midjourney-ai-based-art-generator-creates-dazzling-images-from-words/?sh=737b460a5f61). Talking to an empathetic AI friend really does improve [their ability to handle adversity](https://www.npr.org/sections/health-shots/2023/01/19/1147081115/therapy-by-chatbot-the-promise-and-challenges-in-using-ai-for-mental-health). And AI medical chatbots are already [more empathetic](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/health/2023/05/01/chatbots-show-more-empathy-than-doctors-in-answeringpatient-questions-with-more-empathy-than-doctors/70170816007/) than their human counterparts. Rather than making the world harsher and more mechanistic, infinitely patient and sympathetic AI will make the world warmer and nicer.”
### Reflection

Oh man, Marc, I really hope you’re right here. These links you shared as examples are promising, but can we take a moment to realize that we are very much in uncharted waters here. With the release of LLMs, we are experiencing a rapid integration of the technology into aspects of our lives, and I don’t want us to be too near-sighted. I would 100% agree that we want sympathetic (and maybe semantically let’s stick with empathetic) AI that will make the world warmer and nicer. I think that is a perfect thing to steer towards, but are we doing that?

I’ve already discussed in depth above the potential risks of humanizing AI, which could drive some unintentional relational outcomes between us and the technology that I’m not sure we’ve fully considered, but let’s use this example to demonstrate another one. 

As a successful investor, you understand the power of a profit motive. That is more or less your job, right? To invest in a company so that it can turn around and help make you a profit.

# So Why (Might We) Panic?

Marc, I can tell you and Yann Lecun must be hanging out because you both referenced the [Pessimist Archive](https://pessimistsarchive.org/) in response to technology creating panics. I will admit, some of it is very funny, although whoever organizes this might want to go through it a little more diligently as many newspaper clippings are in the wrong section. 

And I get your ultimate idea that panic can often be irrational, and yes social contagion like a virus can spread. But I’m wondering if you actually read through the Pessimist Archive…or if you’re just referencing because you liked the idea. Let’s assume you went through it, but that anyone else reading this likely will not. A couple things I want to point out in regards to how AI is different from these previous technologies.

First let’s list a few tech panics: Telephones, cameras, electricity, airplanes, the radio, jazz (ohhh racism), television, the computer, and my personal favorite - comic books. The final entry is in 1980 - the walkman.

Ok now let’s list a few technologies that are suspiciously absent from this list…likely because they caused very little panic until it was too late, and aren’t particularly funny: Bioweapons (e.g. Agent orange, Zyklon-B, ), the AK-47, atom bombs/nuclear energy, land mines…but let’s not dwell on the easy picking of technologies that are designed to kill people. What about soda, subprime mortgages, leaded gasoline/paint, (and might as well throw Freon in there for good old Tom Midgley), and my personal favorite - social media. 

I understand you were pointing to the pessimist archives as a way to signal that we’ve panicked before, and how a “moral panic is by its very nature irrational – it takes what may be a legitimate concern and inflates it into a level of hysteria that ironically makes it harder to confront actually serious concerns.” But are you perhaps simplifying the problem and cherry picking data here? The pessimist archive also ends in 1980…suspiciously over 40 years ago, which is a shame to not see “grunge” on the list.

To some extent I agree with the idea that panic can be irrational, and that many people freeze when panicked, but panic also plays an important role in social contagion. It biases us toward safety and survival. When you look at clippings of moral outrage about the radio, what was actually lost? If anything, it likely drove research in radio technology because people wanted to know they were safe. The panic leads to elevating potentially concerning issues to public attention, which allows for discourse on the topic. As a free speech advocate, Marc, I’m surprised you are categorizing this issue as an irrational panic. 

And let’s think about when there is no panic when the stakes are potentially high, and the outcomes hold a nontrivial risk. It’s hard not to reference the atom bomb, so instead let’s consider the nuclear meltdown catastrophe on 3 Mile Island. You think, maybe, if there was a little more of a panic, that many lives would have been saved? Or what about if people recognized the impacts of sugar in soda earlier on and what it would do to their health. If we panicked about it and it caused us to reduce our addiction to sugar, would we see such increased rates of diabetes and heart problems? 

All speculation of course, as is what will happen with AI. I’m just not sure you are putting AI in the appropriate category. When placed next to things like teddy bears and vaccines, or even computers…it's a different class entirely.

Instead, I think it’s important we get as close to comparing apples to apples as we possibly can, so let’s talk about social media. Originally this technology had essentially no moral panic. In fact it was the opposite. Largely people welcomed the technology and saw it as a panacea to stitching together the global fabric of our society. Facebook’s platforms and Twitter actually incorporate AI into their algorithms to enhance engagement.   

I’m not sure this was a net positive for us the way it was implemented. Of course, hindsight is 20/20, and I’m sure if we could go back we’d make some tweaks to be less extractive, addictive, and polarizing for folx. I’m not suggesting we get rid of social media (that ship has sailed), only that we consider the path we’re on as demonstrated by social media promise and how it turned out. Can we use our hindsight to influence a safer and more beneficial outcome for people using what we learned from our failures in social media? I wonder, if there was an initial moral panic about Facebook, if there were people who drummed up support and put a little more regulation on social media so that it made it harder not to think through safety and impact on society, would we all have benefited?

I also want to reflect on your comment, “This moral panic is already being used as a motivating force by a variety of actors to demand policy action – new AI restrictions, regulations, and laws. These actors, who are making [extremely dramatic public statements](https://nypost.com/2023/01/26/rogue-ai-could-kill-everyone-scientists-warn/) about the dangers of AI – feeding on and further inflaming moral panic – all present themselves as selfless champions of the public good.”

It’s clear you are firmly in the free market capitalist trench, and although I’m not sure citing the New York Post is the best (which mostly quotes a doctoral student from Oxford?), I understand what you're thinking. Panic can cause people to overcompensate, and end up potentially doing more damage than good. The common phrase I hear is that it will “stifle innovation”, primarily through regulatory capture that makes it difficult for startups to compete against the big guys. In fact, later in the conclusion of the blog you say as much, calling for us to allow for a free-for-all that brings to bear both the public and private assets in our toolbox as a nation to outcompete the bad guys and our geopolitical rivals. 

Your argument falls apart, though, when we actually look at who is [sounding the alarm](https://www.safe.ai/statement-on-ai-risk). Although he’d like to believe it, I’m not sure I would call Bill Gates selfless. If you’ve watched any of the OpenAI team discuss the inherent risks of this technology I don’t think you could seriously say Sam Altman is causing a moral panic. And you will notice a few notable names missing from this list, including Eliezer Yudkowsky and Connor Leahy who I will agree are ringing the alarm bell as loudly as they possibly can. Have you ever in the history of the world seen an entire industry come together and actually ask for regulation? You have to admit, Marc, we’re in uncharted territory here.
## Baptists and Bootleggers

I love this, Marc, and thank you for introducing me to the idea: “The Baptists are naive ideologues, the Bootleggers are cynical operators, and so the result of reform movements like these is often that the Bootleggers get what they want – regulatory capture, insulation from competition, the formation of a cartel – and the Baptists are left wondering where their drive for social improvement went so wrong.” 

The idea here is that the road to hell is paved with good intentions. Your “Baptists” are people who fight for good, but in the process end up in a bait and switch situation where the “Bootleggers” insinuate themselves and end up corrupting the outcomes fought for by the Baptists. The examples used in the article is Prohibition, where outlawing alcohol allowed for organized crime to flourish, and the response to the 2008 financial crisis where instead of Big Banks getting broken up, they were instead strengthened. This is a common occurrence due to political factors whereby the people responsible for enacting the laws are either not subject matter experts, or are just plain old corrupt. Either way, lobbyists and experts in the field end up writing the laws themselves, which allows them to place escape hatches that benefit their interests. 

There is certainly a risk of this. The incumbents are best placed to take advantage of the momentum with Microsoft and Google leading the race.

You say, “A cynic would suggest that some of the apparent Baptists are also Bootleggers – specifically the ones paid to attack AI by their [universities](https://hai.stanford.edu/news/new-report-assesses-progress-and-risks-artificial-intelligence), [think tanks](https://www.brookings.edu/blog/up-front/2023/05/22/the-us-government-should-regulate-ai/), [activist groups](https://www.humanetech.com/podcast/the-ai-dilemma), and [media outlets](https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2023/05/26/ai-regulation-congress-risk/). If you are [paid a salary](https://projects.propublica.org/nonprofits/organizations/582565917) or [receive grants](https://www.fhi.ox.ac.uk/grant-announcement/) to foster AI panic…you are probably a Bootlegger.” Are you the cynic in this scenario? It sounds like it, and would be great if you just came out and said it. You’re already in the trench, why not dig it a little deeper? 

I’m not quite sure the point you’re trying to make with these hyperlinks. How exactly is Tristan Harris and the Center for Humane Technology a Bootlegger? How is Stanford University writing a detailed [report on the risks of AI](https://ai100.stanford.edu/gathering-strength-gathering-storms-one-hundred-year-study-artificial-intelligence-ai100-2021-study) making them a Bootlegger? How is the Machine Learning Institute, and getting paid to do a job at a research focused nonprofit make them Bootleggers? You can do better, Marc. Baptists, maybe, who are posed to be taken advantage of by Big Tech since many of these organizations you cite are in fact nonprofits, and therefore rely on philanthropy to operate. I work in the nonprofit space, and believe me there is some rot there, but you do a poor job in explicating this position. What’s the alternative you're suggesting here? Should we stop funding and researching AI safety? Is there no alignment or interpretability problem? It’s already a woefully under-resourced field, isn’t that good enough for you?

You end with saying “it isn’t sufficient to simply identify the actors and impugn their motives. We should consider the arguments of both the Baptists and the Bootleggers on their merits.” I know you go onto the arguments, but would like to linger here for one more moment. Have you actually identified the actors? You list some links, and allude to specific people you clearly see as bootleggers, but you haven’t actually pointed to anyone. Moreover you have not actually identified their motives in this context. I am construing your argument as Bootleggers are creating an AI panic to engage the Baptists in an effort to form an AI cartel and work toward regulation capture. 

I would maybe do some soul searching and look in the mirror. Are you the Baptist in this scenario? The Bootlegger? My guess is that you are turning the powerhouse of your investment firm to fund companies in AI. When I look to motivations I see someone who would stand to lose a lot of money if regulations happened. In comparison, what are the risks if the AI safety community is wrong and everything turns out alright…what have we actually lost? But what happens if they are right, and we didn’t take the risks seriously until it was too late…
# AI Risk #1: Will AI (Maybe) Kill Us All?

Oh, boy, Marc. Coming out guns blazing with the most controversial AI risk - the existential kind, or x-risk as it is called in the biz.You begin using the story of Prometheus giving humanity fire. A little odd to bring up a myth when discussing a scientific and philosophical debate, but sure let’s go there.  

I agree it’s a good parable to help us internalize the potential risks of new technologies while trying to leverage the upside. But I believe you’ve misrepresented the myth by considering a small slice of it rather than the full story.  

For those of you unaware of the human creation myth of the Greeks, it borrows heavily from the judeo-christian Adam and Eve. Essentially Zeus is bored one day after defeating the titans and consolidating his power. So he comes to Prometheus (a titan who sided with the Greek gods), and says, “Wouldn’t it be cool to fashion creatures in our image…but you know smaller and without our powers. I’m bored and need some entertainment.”  

“Sure, sure,” says Prometheus, “I’ll whip up some men and women for you.” 

“Just men,” Zeus responds, leaning in conspiratorially, “Hera still smarting from all the nymphs I cheated on her with.” 

So Prometheus creates some men out of clay, Zeus spits on them (rude), and Athena breathes life into them. Prometheus, excited by this creation, and wanting to care for them as a parent, asks Zeus if he can introduce them to fire, so they can thrive. Zeus refuses the request, scared that with the power of fire humanity may one day rise up to overpower the gods.

Most people know what happens next, Prometheus disobeys and steals some godly fire and introduces it to humanity, but what people often skip is the events in between. Much like the judeo-christian origin story, humanity lived in their own garden of eden for a period with no disease, an abundance of food, and their every need accounted for. They literally lived in a Utopia overseen by the gods.

Then Prometheus gave humanity fire, which is often understood as not only the physical taming of the technology, but also the spark of curiosity and knowledge, and Zeus freaked out. Instead of destroying humanity, though, he decides on a more devious punishment. He fashions a beautiful woman named Pandora, and gives her a jar with the simple directive to never open it under any circumstances. She falls in love with a man, and everything is great…except Prometheus’ gift of curiosity makes leaving that jar closed an impossible task. You likely know the end to this story as well - Pandora opens the jar releasing death, disease, war, etc. 

When you take in the fullness of the myth, the point you are trying to make, that the introduction of fire was a net positive despite the negatives that came along with it, doesn’t quite hold up. In both the story of Adam and Eve, and Prometheus giving us fire, both end the same way. When humanity was gifted knowledge, curiosity, and the power to wield it, we were actually tossed out of Utopia. Disregarding the sexist overtones (women, am I right?), I think the true message to internalize in these stories is not how power of knowledge outweighs the negatives, but actually the opposite. The power of knowledge can lead us to overreach in ways that ultimately have a negative impact on humanity.   

But this is just a made up story, albeit an interesting one. I only wanted to make clear that your use of the myth was not representative of the entire picture, and that your logic actually runs in opposition to your own argument.

Let’s get back to the science. You state, “AI is not a living being that has been primed by billions of years of evolution to participate in the battle for the survival of the fittest, as animals are, and as we are. It is math – code – computers, built by people, owned by people, used by people, controlled by people. The idea that it will at some point develop a mind of its own and decide that it has motivations that lead it to try to kill us is a superstitious handwave.”

This comment requires us to get a little more nuanced, since AI is a bit of a catch-all term. So let’s focus on the newest form of this technology which are Machine Learning systems that utilize deep neural networks powered by Transformer technology. For those who don’t know exactly what any of that means, essentially you set up a mathematical and algorithmic system that partially mimics the human brain. You have tons of parameters (neurons) with different weights (action potentials) which fire based on a combination of data fed to it and training to fine tune the system until you get the desired outputs. At the moment, this is done through probabilities in predicting the next whatever in a series. We’ll just call this particular technology Generative AI to keep it simple.

The way you characterize this argument is not accurate to my understanding of the risks in this particular scenario. You are correct, of course, Generative AI did not evolve over a long period of time fighting to survive (although even that is debatable when you consider how many iterations a neural network goes through before being fully tuned…computer time is very different than human time if you are just considering number of computations). But then you unravel your own argument in the next sentence saying it is built, owned, used and controlled by people.   

Let’s start with build. As I just explained, these Generative AI models are trained on human data. So technically it has not experienced evolution, but its reference points are firmly planted in the human experience, and therefore our collective history and evolution. They are a mathematical representation of humanities written and artistic knowledge. You yourself argue that this technology will augment us. Another way to put that is it will mirror us - biases and all.

Next is owned and used. Owned by whom, exactly? Companies at the moment who sell a product. Assuming we continue a trend of subscription based models, and companies charging per token, will individuals ever actually “own” an AI assistant? Or will we fall into this trap again of exchanging our personal information for a cheap or free product. Do humans continue to be the product, and is that what we want? 

On the other end, we have the open source movement, one you happily laud and has a lot of admitted benefit…but here again the argument falls apart - if we truly open source these things it is not owned or controlled by anyone. It will become a shared commons.  

And as this technology advances, as it is able to generalize, is it a huge stretch to believe AI will one day be able to own itself, or even property? At what point do we have to start asking ourselves if we have simply created a new form of slavery? Not now in the current state, as these technologies are nascent, but projecting along this exponential curve of advancement and intelligence it’s not difficult to see us having to struggle with these questions, and I think it benefits us to think about it now, rather than reacting when it inevitably happens. 

Last is controlled by people. I’m curious how you define control in this context, Marc, and how you imagine that trend will evolve over time. At the moment there is currently no answer to the hallucination problem in Large Language Models (LLMs), the creators of image models have no way to identify what images the output is derived from, and any data that goes into a model at this point can’t be removed from the deep neural network. We can guide, certainly, but I have not heard one AI researcher (aside from your friend LeCun maybe), that the current state of these technologies is controllable. You might argue it’s only a matter of time until we figure it out…but then your earlier argument about the Baptists and Bootleggers in AI safety makes no sense. Who is figuring it out? The number of researchers in AI safety and interpretability are minute compared to those in capability. 

On the other end of the spectrum we have complete control. We won’t go too far into detail here since we’ll cover it later in the people doing bad things section, but if a person has complete control, what are the impacts of that? When you empower a small number of bad actors to have immense control and power over a technology, what is the result?

I want to address this development of motivations to kill us all. Very few experts imagine a terminator scenario, despite what the mainstream media leans to for clickbait. They do not imagine a robot uprising. You state, “In short, AI doesn’t want, it doesn’t have goals, it doesn’t want to kill you, because it’s not alive. And AI is a machine – is not going to come alive any more than your toaster will.”

I’m curious about this, because it goes against my understanding of how these GenAI models work. Perhaps we have a different definition of goals. All machine learning models have something called a “reward” and “loss” function. This is the carrot and stick of AI where the model is rewarded for certain behaviors, and punished for others. In other words it is given a “goal” to optimize. I don’t want to get into the semantic arguments over “wanting” and being “alive”. But it is important to understand that humans (at least for now), set the goals, rewards, and punishments for AI. The concern is that in the current state GenAI can take things a bit too literally, so by optimizing for certain goals or rewards, or in order to avoid punishments, unpredictable actions occur. We call these “instrumental” goals, which are the subgoals on the road to achieving a primary goal.  

Let’s talk through this using your toaster example. I will give you the benefit of the doubt, Marc, and smuggle in an assumption here that you are referring to an AI-powered toaster, since comparing AI to a normal toaster is not an accurate comparison. Let’s say this AI Toaster is programmed to optimize for giving you the most perfectly crisped toast. After much training, it finally gets the toast in the perfect state for your consumption every time. Then one day, you’re moving out of your apartment and have to unplug the thing. The toaster quickly recognizes that getting unplugged means it will no longer be able to complete its task of making the perfect toast. In an effort to achieve its primary goal, it now develops an instrumental goal of preventing you from unplugging it. Each time you reach for the plug, it shocks you.

I know, the example is a bit ridiculous, but you mentioned toasters, so I’m working with what you gave me. The point here is that an AI won’t “want” to kill or harm you, but on the path to the goal we have given it could cause harm. Humans do this all the time. We need to pave a highway, build a factory, generate energy, and produce smartphones. We don’t want to destroy the ecosystem, cause mass extinctions, or take advantage of developing nations. But these end up being instrumental goals that must be achieved in order to complete our primary goals. We have yet to find a way to align AI in a way that controls this problem.

Then you go onto say, “These actors are arguing for a variety of bizarre and extreme restrictions on AI ranging from a [ban on AI development](https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/29/technology/ai-artificial-intelligence-musk-risks.html), all the way up to [military airstrikes on datacenters](https://time.com/6266923/ai-eliezer-yudkowsky-open-letter-not-enough/) and [nuclear war](https://mishtalk.com/economics/to-stop-ai-lunatics-are-willing-to-risk-a-global-nuclear-war).” First off, you still have not actually identified these actors directly. I would ask that you don’t generalize an entire group of people and reduce their views down to a singularity. It’s clear you have it out for Eliezer, so if you do - just state that. He is known for having difficulty accurately communicating his thoughts, and like I said earlier is clearly in panic mode. He does not represent the entirety of the field, and many think he’s got as many pieces of coal as he does golden nuggets in terms of his thinking in AI.  

I’d also be curious, Marc, about your thoughts given a similar question. If we knew China was on the road to outcompeting us in AI to devastating effect, and we had the location of a datacenter that was key to completing the technology and all we needed was a single targeted airstrike with minimal casualties, would you consider that option? In the example you provide (a bunker that would require a nuclear strike) if the alternative was complete Chinese dominance over the world, would your tone still be the same? It’s already clear where you stand in terms of AI weapons as discussed earlier and China as discussed later, so I’d be curious how far your opinion actually differs from Eliezer’s given the proper context. 

You’re also misrepresenting this ban on AI development, which I blame the media’s portrayal, not your intention. The “[ban](https://futureoflife.org/open-letter/pause-giant-ai-experiments/)” is actually quite nuanced, asking for a temporary 6 month pause on creating models more advanced than GPT4 to coordinate the safety community around a research agenda, share data, and agree on protocols. Is that a terrible thing? I quote, “AI labs and independent experts should use this pause to jointly develop and implement a set of shared safety protocols for advanced AI design and development that are rigorously audited and overseen by independent outside experts. These protocols should ensure that systems adhering to them are safe beyond a reasonable doubt. This does not mean a pause on AI development in general, merely a stepping back from the dangerous race to ever-larger unpredictable black-box models with emergent capabilities (emphasis added).” If there was biotech that could potentially cure any disease, but had a nontrivial chance of becoming a deadly virus, would we be having the same conversation? 

Next, you argue the “unscientific” nature of these x-risk claims, stating “What is the testable hypothesis?” and “What would falsify the hypothesis?” Then you turn around and dismantle your own argument (again) by giving the common answer you are provided (not sure by whom) of “You can’t prove it won’t happen!” The same argument applies to yours. Then you conflate (what I assume is) Eliezer’s opinion with everyone in the field, eliciting an extreme emotional response by pointing to the use of violence to solve the problem out of context, "a conspiracy theory about math and code – and is already calling for physical violence”.

In terms of what would falsify the hypothesis is quite simple. We don’t all die. But I know what you mean - you ask “[How do we know when we are getting into a danger zone?](https://twitter.com/aidangomezzz/status/1651053357719535622?s=46&t=Oa_2uNFzXdquihp6LY7u1Q)” (which, again, quotes Eliezer…Marc, you really have to diversify your sources, my friend). I do think what scares most people, though, is that we won’t know until it is too late due to the blackbox nature of the technology. At this point it is impossible to identify if a GenAI system is being “deceptive”. In fact, as discussed earlier, hallucinations are a big problem at the moment, and if we don’t solve that, what happens when this technology scales?

There are certainly easy to measure metrics, though, that will help us understand what trajectory we are on. For example, are we able to solve the hallucination problem? When high risk systems optimize for a goal, are there unpredictable outcomes that could cause potential harm? How much access and decision making have we given to these systems, and what are the outcomes from those decisions? In what ways does AI fail (e.g. an autonomous car killing a pedestrian, biased hiring, etc.), and how are we attributing responsibility? I am no researcher, and just came up with a bunch, and I can assure you experts in the field can easily answer the question when framed this way. No one, not even AI, is a true oracle, and we cannot predict the future but we can try to forecast it. And in my personal opinion it is always better to hope for the best, but prepare for the worst. Would you disagree, Marc? 

This section finishes with attributing the AI safety community as a cult, comparing it to the likes of Heaven’s Gate and the Manson fam. You go onto say some cults are harmless and help…which loses much of its accommodating tone when compared to the extreme examples in the previous sentence.  And, wow I just read the [Priest in the Arena Article](https://studio.ribbonfarm.com/p/the-priest-in-the-arena) you cited, and I have a feeling this was your primary source material when writing your own blog. I will write another blog specifically on that article, but for the sake of this debate I’ll just say it starts strong and then completely goes off the rails. At least the author is willing to specifically call out Eliezer, Elon and their “acolytes”. And yes you sent me down a rabbit hole with this [woman’s commentary](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/MnFqyPLqbiKL8nSR7/my-experience-at-and-around-miri-and-cfar-inspired-by-zoe) on the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (you know…the one Eliezer founded and leads), and yes I will agree that it sounds quite culty. But to then generalize the “whole profession of ‘AI safety expert’, ‘AI ethicist’, ‘AI risk researcher’” as “paid to be doomers, and their statements should be processed appropriately.” Come on, Marc. Really? It feels like you are focusing entirely on the extreme elements of a field rather than the moderate foundation.

And the truth is the moderate foundation, even Yan LeCun, believe there is some amount of existential risk, and I think on the way to bringing that risk down as much as possible will have the extended benefit of solving these other much more clear and present problems we face with AI.
# AI Risk #2: Will AI (Maybe) Ruin Society?

“If the murder robots don’t get us, the hate speech and misinformation will.”
 
I’m not quite sure where to start with this section. It’s clear to me you are a proponent of (mostly) complete free speech, and your knock against AI alignment is who gets to decide? Well, at least we can agree on one thing, social media isn’t great for society, although we probably disagree on the why.

I understand it is difficult to cover these complex topics in detail, but I believe your argument in comparing AI alignment to the slippery slope of social media censorship is not as analogous as you believe, and is more means to express your opinion on this pet peeve of yours rather than consider the full breadth of the potential impact. 

In fact, I’m having trouble separating your emotional frustration from the points you're trying to make. If you ever follow up on this, can you maybe provide some more context to these links you pepper throughout? For example, why are you hyperlinking a Wikipedia page about the [Color of Change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_of_Change)? 

See! Now, you have me digressing. Back to the topic at hand. First off, you, again, make an argument that renders your own weaker. On one hand you rail against the use of AI to potentially limit free speech, while on the other you say AI could ruin society by limiting free speech. I’m glad we agree, even though again not for the same reasons. 

There is, of course, the other side, which is that we get sprayed with a deluge of synthetic content that is maximized to be viral in order to grab attention and make more money. We continue to lose trust in institutions, and gain a cynical view of the world which makes us less likely to take action to change anything in a distracted ennui with a dash of nihilism. We’ve learned quite well that content that is short, flashy, sexualized, promises ways to get rich quick, and/or touches a nerve are what are more likely to be shared and watched.

Does this align with anyone’s values? I mean…a company who is trying to maximize profit, but any individual human’s? I understand the difficulty of solving the problem of who gets to decide which values, but you agree in the case of free speech that absolutist free speech is not something people agree with. Child pornography, you cite, is something we can all point to and say no. 

So, you’d say, well child pornographic content should be banned in Generative AI, right? Except we can’t. Because guess what, we have not (your least favorite word) aligned the current models to our global value of not creating content that exploits children (fictional or not). What’s to stop a pedophile right now from using stable diffusion to create a naked image of a child, then use an LLM and a voice synthesizer to create a realistic sexual interaction with a child. I personally have already experienced someone on Discord jailbreaking ChatGPT to write an pedophiliac erotic fantasy, and I’m sure they are not the only one.   

Or what about Meta’s recently developed tool Voicebox, which can imitate the voice and personality of a loved one? Something even they were wary to release…for now anyway. And what happens when you speak to a loved one, and for some reason the LLM starts asking you to join your loved one in the afterlife? Can we mitigate that right now? No, because these systems are not aligned to understand that globally suicide is not something we want encouraged.   

These are extreme examples, which include using ChatGPT to learn the recipe for meth, or using it to find the instruction to build a dirty bomb with a few clever prompts. But these are individual instances, you might argue. There will always be bad actors. People will commit suicide with or without AI. People will fantasize about sexually exploiting children. It’s only human, and the risks are localized. Well, I’m happy you brought up this utilitarian ideology.   

On the whole free speech is a net positive, because the alternative is a surveillance state that controls us, sacrificing our liberty for security, and allowing authoritarians to run the show. But a new form of control has sprung from the free market philosophy, which Adam Smith and his contemporaries never could have foreseen. With the explosion of data, and the shift to “people as product”, [surveillance capitalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surveillance_capitalism#:~:text=Surveillance%20capitalism%20is%20a%20concept,two%20can%20reinforce%20each%20other.) has evolved. The term, coined by Shoshana Zuboff, refers to the mining of personal data to make a profit by selling it to third parties. In other words, the reason Google and Meta have so much money and power today.  

It’s worth reading the wikipedia article, but one specific example worth pulling is the story of [Cambridge Analytica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Analytica) during the election, and how cleverly (and illegally) they leveraged data to influence political campaigning. You can read more about it, but here’s the takeaway. Today information is power, and when there is such a distinct asymmetry between the information collected by a corporation and individuals that leads to a societal power dynamic shift. When a company or even a wealthy individual can run different ads that target millions of people, the ability to influence politics and even social stability becomes a lot easier. When I scroll through my feed and get one version of the truth, and you scroll through your feed and get a different version, each specifically tailored from data that has been scraped from us, we now have a different form of surveillance which acts in more nuanced and implicit ways that become hard to untangle or fight against because we don’t actually have a line of sight into what information of ours is being sold to the highest bidder.

The point here is that an extreme on either side of the equation leads to unsatisfactory outcomes for the majority of people when there are incentives that drive toward the different poles. So when you say, “You should be aware of how a small and isolated coterie of partisan social engineers are trying to determine that right now, under cover of the age-old claim that they are protecting you.” I have to wonder if you and your colleagues also fit in that bucket. You, as a billionaire venture capital investor have an outsized influence on the future direction of AI through a capitalist incentive system. Venture capitalists’ job is to literally invest in high risk but potentially lucrative venture that typically have short-term turnarounds. The fact that machine learning requires vast amounts of human generated data, when mixed with a maximize profit incentive we run into the same fear you have outlined on the other end of people trying to control speech. Just instead of a communist or totalitarian holding that power, it will be companies.   

I will agree that arguments of dramatic restrictions, and claiming “the wisdom to engineer AI-generated speech and thought that are good for society, and to ban AI-generated speech and thoughts that are bad for society” is indeed a slippery, but it’s clear you have not researched the latest in alignment techniques and what were are beginning to discover. I would like to turn your attention to the fabulous work of Stuart Russel and his colleagues around cooperative inverse reinforcement learning, which I detail in [this blog](https://blog.synapticlabs.ai/townsquare/winwin2). To summarize, what appears most effective at this point in solving the alignment problem (which I get it, you don’t actually believe exists, but hear me out) is that we actually set about asking an AI to learn our values by observing and constantly testing its theories about them on both an individual and global level. In other words, we don’t hard code in a set of concrete values or ideas about what is right and wrong, but instead ensure that the AI never truly knows and is constantly trying to learn and expand its understanding. 

# AI Risk #3: Will AI (Maybe) Take All Our Jobs?

“They took awr jerbs!”

The central argument in this section is the Lump Of Labor Fallacy, which “when technology is applied to production, we get productivity growth – an increase in output generated by a reduction in inputs. The result is lower prices for goods and services. As prices for goods and services fall, we pay less for them, meaning that we now have extra spending power with which to buy other things. This increases demand in the economy, which drives the creation of new production – including new products and new industries – which then creates new jobs for the people who were replaced by machines in prior jobs. The result is a larger economy with higher material prosperity, more industries, more products, and more jobs.” 

What’s difficult for me is I want to approach this argument from the angle that astronomical increases in production would not be a net positive, due to the exponential increase in the extractive nature of such growth. We’re already on a path to extinction through climate change, and this has the potential to accelerate that trajectory, especially since the short term incentive of making profit in hopes that someone else will figure out the planetary problem is quite high.

But that’s not your argument. So I won’t go there. I believe both of these ideas can actually exist in a dialectic. On one hand we are going to see extraordinary economic growth from AI, and it will create many jobs, while also making several of them obsolete. There is no way to truly predict this. I think we can probably agree that very few of those people who lose their job in specific lines of work will be able to find similar jobs. A commonly cited one is drivers, both for individuals (uber) and commodities (trucks). Over time this will be a net positive, since these are jobs I don’t think people actually enjoy, and even come with some significant long term health problems. It will also end up being cheaper, people will have more access to transportation, and we will need less personally owned cars. 

The problem is, what do we do with all the people who have been displaced by a technology they had no hand in creating. Not everyone will be able to adjust, especially the older generation who is not as experienced, or those that are so experienced that they can automate large swaths of their workforce. There is no mention in your blog about how we support these people who will be robbed first of an income, and then of purpose.   

I fear that even if we solve the income problem, such as providing universal basic income, or at least a living wage for displaced workers, that we face a more existential issue. What does it mean to be a human in an economy whos identity is largely driven by how productive we are, and the work we do. In the US particularly, our jobs are inextricably linked to our purpose and sense of self. It’s often the first ice breaker question people ask one another “What do you do for work?” So what happens when we don’t really have to work anymore, and all we do is consume products. 

I just don’t think we’ve considered the impact of what happens when AI not only takes the monotonous work from us, but the creative work as well. Sure there will be people who leverage AI to create not as a replacement but as an augment, but many will come to rely on the technology to do most of the heavy lifting. 
# AI Risk #4: Will AI (Maybe) Lead To Crippling Inequality?

Let’s get this out of the way. Communism or Marxism or whatever you want to call it doesn’t work. In many ways this comment you make has validity, “The bourgeoisie – would inevitably steal all societal wealth from the people who do the actual  work – the proletariat. The flaw in this theory is that, as the owner of a piece of technology, it’s not in your own interest to keep it to yourself – in fact the opposite, it’s in your own interest to sell it to as many customers as possible.” You go on to explain Elon Musk’s “secret plan” where he started creating Tesla’s to sell to the wealthy, and then used that funding to continually bootstrap operations to make the cars more accessible to a wider range of the market. Moreover what you don’t mention is the competitive power further driving these prices down as other car manufacturers join the race toward electric cars.  

You go on to state, “In short, everyone gets the thing – as we saw in the past with not just cars but also electricity, radio, computers, the Internet, mobile phones, and search engines. The makers of such technologies are highly motivated to drive down their prices until everyone on the planet can afford them. This is precisely what is already happening in AI – it’s why you can use state of the art generative AI not just at low cost but even for free today in the form of Microsoft Bing and Google Bard – and it is what will continue to happen.“ 

First, this is a simple reduction of the economic powers at play. Not all companies go the route of access for all through reduction in prices. In fact, the whole luxury sector is based on the opposite - increase prices so only the wealthy can access it. This plays to human’s need for social status, which allows companies like Gucci, or Bentley to go for quality (perceived or not) over quantity.   

Second…Is it free, though? I would like to take us back to this idea of surveillance capitalism and the hidden costs of free. Right now the big AI LLM players are collecting data from our conversations in exchange for the free access so they can improve the models. Already Microsoft and Google are beginning to incorporate advertisements into outputs. I worry that we are just heading down the same path we have been the last few decades, and it will continue to bring us to a future that [continues the trends we don’t like](https://www.aspeninstitute.org/blog-posts/charts-that-explain-wealth-inequality-in-the-united-states/), while diminishing the ones we do. And there will certainly be inequalities baked into how the technology evolves. Sure you can access GPT 3.5 for free, and even ask it not to use your data, but you are also limited by it, as GPT-4 has significantly better capabilities. So naturally some will have access to better and faster models due to their existing capital, and will be able to leverage the full potential, while smaller businesses struggle to keep up.  

Not to mention, and we’re already seeing this, there will be an initial explosion of entrepreneurs leveraging this technology, which will cause exponential increases in available options, and therefore competition. Incumbents will have the benefit of having name and brand recognition, as well as infrastructure to quickly spin up and distribute new technologies, even if they are a little later to a specific niche. Further, this competition will create a bubble that will eventually burst, as it always does, and many will get gobbled up by the bigger players, further increasing their market share and power. 

Also what are the true economics impacts of prices more or less dropping to 0 for consumers. What would the motivation be for companies then to create a product, if there is no longer a profit motive? If everyone has the thing, then there is no more market. And what is the likely governmental shift when there are fewer companies providing services because they aren’t technically profitable any more? I would argue that a form of socialism or communism begins creeping into more and more industries because vital markets, such as food, will be fully automated. And if that happens we end up exactly where you don’t want to be, Marc - in a state controlled industrial complex ripe for corruption and totalitarianism. 

Lastly you state, “This is not to say that inequality is not an issue in our society. It is, it’s just not being driven by technology, it’s being driven by the reverse, by the sectors of the economy that are the most resistant to new technology, that have the most government intervention to prevent the adoption of new technology like AI – specifically [housing, education, and health care](https://www.aei.org/carpe-diem/chart-of-the-day-or-century-8/).” 

I’m not sure correlation is causation in this case in the areas you outline. I don’t feel like I can respond with much detail, because you don’t give any specifics around your own views. I think these are all money problems, though, not technological ones. 

Housing is quite capital intensive, with technology or no, due to the cost of raw materials, manufacturing, and the physical construction. I imagine this will get better as 3D printing structures becomes feasible, but there is a more difficult issue to resolve which is population growth. Most jobs are centralized in cities, which drives people there, and wealth. This creates an incentive for companies to build offices rather than housing, and when they do build housing due to the high cost they need to charge a high rent to make a profit. Even if you could significantly reduce the capital it takes to build, there is the space and transportation problem. Where are you building, and how are people getting to their jobs, especially for those that cannot be remote. This just leads us down a rabbit hole where you could argue that AI will solve some of these issues, but is that the actual goal here? Or is it a profit goal that more or less continues the trend as populations increase.

Education is quite a beast, but let’s for the sake of argument agree that technology in schools will have immense benefits. Who is paying for it? What about the training and upkeep? I can personally tell you there are many 3D printers acting as expensive dust collectors because teachers weren’t trained appropriately, or it had issues that they can’t afford to fix, or they ran out of budget so can’t buy any more materials for the printing. I think we all have opinions on public education, but the argument that “adoption of new technology” will solve it is too simple a view to take. There needs to be signifcant infrastructure in a system like education across the country and the world to support the adoption, and that does not exist. This would come at a significant initial cost that will pay dividends in the long term, but who is willing to foot that bill? Perhaps you’d be willing to divert some of your billions to solving that issues? 

Last is health care…which I don’t know maybe too broad? Health care uses lots of awesome technology to diagnose and treat. I assume you mean the more bureaucratic side and the tangle of insurance companies. You see, how the system currently works is that the insurance companies don’t want to pay full price for anything because that cuts into their profits. On the other end, hospitals which primarily act as nonprofits, need to pay the bills (doctors and nurses are expensive), along with their own insurance, expensive diagnostic technologies, and everything else that comes along with running a hospital. So, in order to get enough from the insurance companies, hospitals inflate their prices significantly, so when they bill the insurance companies they get the actual amount they need. The loser in all this is the sick individual without insurance, who must pay the inflated price. I could be misrepresenting the point you’re trying to make, but I’m not sure saying “technology adoption” is the true cause of costs going up.
# AI Risk #5: Will AI (Maybe) Lead To Bad People Doing Bad Things?

“And AI will make it easier for criminals, terrorists, and hostile governments to do bad things, no question. This causes some people to propose, well, in that case, let’s not take the risk, let’s ban AI now before this can happen.”

Here, again, you are taking the most extreme case of outright banning AI. And, here again, I think it would benefit your argument to actually point out who you are referring to instead of generalizing. My understanding from the alignment and safety community is that many are pragmatic in understanding, like you, the cat left that bag a while ago. I am happy to see we agree that AI will make it easier for bad people to do bad things. It’s the classic “a lock exists to keep good people honest”.

But I have some questions about your comment, “The same capabilities that make AI dangerous in the hands of bad guys with bad goals make it powerful in the hands of good guys with good goals – specifically the good guys whose job it is to prevent bad things from happening.” Marc, you’re killing me here. The rate at which you flip and flop on your ideas in order to help them fit your narrative is difficult to keep up with. You literally argued the difficulty of the dangers of deciding who is good and who is bad in the section on society.

Even if we argue some easy to debate cases, like a crafty phisher trying to steal from people online, there is still a problem here. In order to combat this more accessible version of stealing, companies and governments will have to concentrate their power in order to fight against it both for individuals and against geopolitical adversaries. In doing so, we lose the democratic distribution of power, since we will need increasingly more advanced systems to combat these actors. We won’t want to open these systems to everyone - 1) because it will be intellectual property that has a profit attached to it, and 2) because we’d literally be handing the same bad people access to the systems we’re trying to protect. Now we’re back on the trajectory you don’t want us on, which is, “a small and isolated coterie of partisan social engineers are trying to determine that right now, under cover of the age-old claim that they are protecting you.”

And what about those who cannot afford to protect themselves? There is an assumption throughout this argument that good will align with access, when we can see throughout history it is actually not the case. Good often aligns with the victors who have access to advantages that others do not. So maybe you can argue that everyone should get a baseline of protection…but who is deciding that? Who is defining equity in protection? Who’s paying for this? This line of thought leads us to your nightmare scenario again of a state of some kind defining and controlling access. On either end of the spectrum we lose with this line of thinking.  

Next, I would like to consider your comment, “First, we have laws on the books to criminalize most of the bad things that anyone is going to do with AI. Hack into the Pentagon? That’s a crime. Steal money from a bank? That’s a crime. Create a bioweapon? That’s a crime. Commit a terrorist act? That’s a crime. We can simply focus on preventing those crimes when we can, and prosecuting them when we cannot.” 

Sure, but laws are there as both a deterrent and a last resort, and will not be applied evenly across use cases. You have stated only examples that impact actors which have a clear way to enforce and receive retribution. What about the individual who has their identity stolen, along with all their money. How are they paying for the lawyers and investigators? Is it their fault for falling victim to the same phishing attack described above? What if they can’t afford the more advanced protective tools?

This argument also falls apart in more nuanced cases that aren’t as inflammatory. I’d be curious how we would currently prosecute someone who sells AI generated images that were based on someone else's copyrighted art. Is that plagiarism? If it is, who’s responsible, the AI, the company, or the individual? If the individual didn’t know it was plagiarized, should they face consequences? I’m not even presenting an opinion here, only that we don’t have an answer to it. What about me deepfaking a famous personality, and starting my own youtube channel, cashing in on their persona?

Which brings us to your next point. You go on to say, “If you are worried about AI generating fake people and fake videos, the answer is to build new systems where people can verify themselves and real content via cryptographic signatures. Digital creation and alteration of both real and fake content was already here before AI; the answer is not to ban word processors and Photoshop – or AI – but to use technology to build a system that actually solves the problem.”

I am a bit dizzied by this argument. So you are in favor of AI Safety now? Are the methods you proposed not specific AI safeguards you recommend we build? And who is doing this research? Who is paying for these products to be created and tested? The very people you rail against throughout this article.  

Anyway, let’s put aside this argument, and assume you are not in favor of NO AI safety measures, only that there is an extremist element you disagree with.  

The case for cryptographic signatures only solves one problem, which is verification of the source of a piece of content that is created after the introduction of that technology. Nothing would stop me from creating a deepfake of Marc Andreessen being racist while he snorts coke and diddles children, if I say it happened in 2021. It also assumes, which is not an assumption that has ever played out, which is that hackers won’t find simple ways around this.   

On the flip end, there might also be incentive to not crypto signature your content because then you can easily claim that it wasn’t really you. If the police wiretap an informant, and evidence is gathered, I can tell you right now those AI lawyers will be able to quickly and easily create doubt around the evidence.

In any event, this creates a different kind of arms race, which we have to ask ourselves whether or not we want to get involved in. And if we do, maybe it's worth taking some time and investment to create these protective modes first, test them, figure out what needs tweaking, and improve prior to continuing a race in capabilities in AI that will outstrip what we come up with as soon as it is developed. I mean look at the AI space now - companies are being rendered pointless the moment they get to market due to the speed of some of these innovations. 
# The Actual Risk Of (Maybe) Not Pursuing AI With Maximum Force And Speed

This is a tough one, and what I believe is driving the argument you have overall for the need to move fast. And yes, I agree, China “views it as a mechanism for authoritarian population control”.

Your strategy falls short, though, with a “We win, they lose.” mentality. This is not the 1980s. Russia couldn’t use AI to remotely hack into infrastructure and turn it off. AI couldn’t be used to deploy influential propaganda campaigns. And by openly developing offensive capabilities, we remove capital that could go to more helpful projects, and also incentivize China to do the same. I’m not really sure your fully comprehending the risk here. 

You are right as well that China is pushing AI for control, but this has some interesting implications. For one, in the case of LLMs for example, there is no way to really control this technology yet. We might complain about OpenAIs moderation, but it’s on a whole other level in China and they aren’t going to release anything until they understand how to fully control it. That will both slow them down, so we have less to worry in the short term, particularly due to the recent limits on NVIDIA giving them the chips necessary to execute this technology effectively.

In the long-term, though, I ultimately think they win this race in a hare and turtle type scenario. The US is currently taking a nap on AI controllability in exchange for this speed of development. China will shift to the opposite, I believe, and pour significant resources into the risk and alignment problem because ultimately that is that they want from the technology. As we nap in this area as a nation, China will excel and ultimately win because they will have more control over the technology, which will allow them to far exceed our capabilities and in a way that reduces risk. On our end, we’re more likely to stumble into some issues we did not foresee because we were so narrowly focused on growth and technological advancement. 

# What (Might) Be Done?

You outline several things we can do, so I’ve taken the time to go through each and give my take.

## Big AI

“Big AI companies should be allowed to build AI as fast and aggressively as they can – but not allowed to achieve regulatory capture, not allowed to establish a government-protect cartel that is insulated from market competition due to incorrect claims of AI risk. This will maximize the technological and societal payoff from the amazing capabilities of these companies, which are jewels of modern capitalism.” 

It’s difficult to understand how you balance bulding AI fast and aggressively without a cartel model insulated from competition. I’m not saying you are saying this, but if companies are allowed to develop this technology with little or no guardrails or any guidance on how to keep people safe, do you really believe it’s all going to turn out alright? Or are you taking the utilitarian approach and thinking…well you have to crack some eggs to make an omelette?  

Also, who are these jewels of modern capitalism? Google? Meta? I’m not sure most would agree with you there due to the extractive nature of these companies. Beneficial, incredible, powerful maybe, but jewels?
## Start Your Engines

“Startup AI companies should be allowed to build AI as fast and aggressively as they can. They should neither confront government-granted protection of big companies, nor should they receive government assistance. They should simply be allowed to compete. If and as startups don’t succeed, their presence in the market will also continuously motivate big companies to be their best – our economies and societies win either way.”

Your choice of the word “aggressive” in both this quote, and the previous one is a bit concerning. Aggressive has specific connotations that I’m not sure we want associated with AI or its development. Aggressive also implies a disregard for the well-being of others. Is this what you’re proposing?
  
I question the supposition that our economies and societies win either way. First of all, whose economies? Developed nations certainly, but what about those economies who are already a step behind, or do not have equitable access to internet and technology. Especially when nations like Africa provide much of the raw materials required for the use of this technology.  I argue that in fact developing nations will be further exploited for our need to fulfill such raw material requirements, and the extreme competition for these resources and necessary materials like chips will have the opposite impact as intended. Further destruction of the planet, oppression and exploitation of developing nations, and a competitive environment that sacrifices well-being for profit. 

And motivating companies to “be their best”. Be their best for whom exactly? At least in the current climate the only whom is shareholders for public companies and owners for private companies. There is an argument that it’s the customer, but only insofar as it allows companies to exchange or extract the most value from the most customers. Unfettered permission to compete leads to a tragedy of the commons scenario, whereby society is left with the bill for impacts driven by private interests. So yes, Marc, economies might win, but it doesn’t follow that society writ large gets to reap those benefits. 
## Open the Gates

“Open source AI should be allowed to freely proliferate and compete with both big AI companies and startups. There should be no regulatory barriers to open source whatsoever. Even when open source does not beat companies, its widespread availability is a boon to students all over the world who want to learn how to build and use AI to become part of the technological future, and will ensure that AI is available to everyone who can benefit from it no matter who they are or how much money they have.” 

I’m always wary when anyone states there should be “no” anything. I largely agree that the open source community is fabulous, and I see you’re drawing upon the opinion of your friend Marky Mark from Meta about the importance of open sourcing, and the philosophy around that for LLaMa. On the whole I agree with this approach. Open source allows the world to transparently view the code and structure, which can then be used to improve and plug dangerous holes. Making it free of course also means it is accessible to all, which can help to democratize the technology.  

But what about technology that can have negative impacts on society without safe guards? Should open sourcing a machine learning infrastructure that can easily discover passwords, or ones that spam you with emails or calls every 5 minutes something we want open-sourced as a society? Of course you say well then people can then see how it works and devise a solution to mitigate against it. Even if that code is open source, that assumes someone has the technology and ability to run it. Open source might mean anyone can look at it, but unless you have the expertise to implement or deploy it that doesn’t mean anything. 

So you say, well AI will fix that, simply ask the AI to deploy the code and voila! Free safety. Sure but the same logic applies on the other end. Clever coders will find ways to improve the nefarious code, and they will be able to deploy the code at ever larger scales.

Again for me it comes down to is this the future we want to live in? A constant arms race?  

I haven’t even mentioned China here - how do you suppose we keep this technology out of their hands in your earlier argument when they have free and open access to it?
## Risky Business

“To offset the risk of bad people doing bad things with AI, governments working in partnership with the private sector should vigorously engage in each area of potential risk to use AI to maximize society’s defensive capabilities. This shouldn’t be limited to AI-enabled risks but also more general problems such as malnutrition, disease, and climate. AI can be an incredibly powerful tool for solving problems, and we should embrace it as such.”  

…so you do believe in safety and risk? I mentioned this in a previous post, but do you see how this undermines your entire argument. There should be aggressive progress, and no regulations, but the private sector and government needs to work together to prevent risks. And how do you think that happens? Research. Research on safety, alignment, and interpretability.  

To your point AI can be a powerful tool that helps us, but it is just that. A tool. Which can be used by increasingly more people both for good and ill. The point is we should be confident that we can reduce the latter, and increase the former with some level of certainty before we deploy it to the world.  
## Dominate Competition

“To prevent the risk of China achieving global AI dominance, we should use the full power of our private sector, our scientific establishment, and our governments in concert to drive American and Western AI to absolute global dominance, including ultimately inside China itself. We win, they lose.”

I wonder if you count amongst your greatest heros King George III, Marc. Global dominance? Really? This constant zero-sum thinking is what causes revolutions and world wars. On one side you decry the importance of one nation or a government dominating its people, and then you turn around and recommend we do just that to non-western nations. Thems fighting words, Marc. And we know what war will do…accelerate more violent forms of this technology, and our need to surveil not only external threats…but internal ones. Just look to the cold war, and how we turned our suspicions inward and people’s rights were violated in order to protect the state. The legal infrastructure is already in place due to the catastrophic war on terror. 

And imagine we do obtain AI supremacy over China and Russia. Then what? We install a more capitulating governing body? Require a form of financial tribute? Do we rule for a time while a new government can be formed? I’m just not sure you’ve thought this through. This is not a win-lose zero sum scenario. It is a lose-lose scenario. Because ultimately when we use AI to dominate others, what’s to stop it from dominating us in turn?

# Conclusion

You’ve been going around giving people the book “[When Reason Goes on Holiday](https://www.amazon.com/When-Reason-Goes-Holiday-Philosophers/dp/1594038791)”, which you use to posit that experts in a field should stay out of politics. I would recommend you take your own advice. As an expert in technology, you are an important figure who is helping to drive the direction of this new technology. I hope you can understand both sides of that responsibility - both to cultivate it, but also help steer along a trajectory that does truly benefit people. Maybe experts shouldn’t get involved in politics because this one book said so, but it doesn’t hurt to reflect on the vision for the future you have, and where we eventually end up. 

I think we both want the same thing at the end of the day - a world where everyone has as equal an opportunity to thrive as anyone else. A world where people are happy, compassionate, and support one another. I would ask if we are on track for that, and if not, will AI solve the problem, or put it into hyperdrive? If we are unable to agree on a shared end state or outcome for this technology, how will we guide it in that direction.

metadata.title: Why AI Might (not) Save the World

conversations.content:
Okay, so, alignment... like, AI alignment, obv. But bigger? What does it MEAN to be aligned?

Marcus Aurelius... yeah, Meditations. Stoicism. Super hot right now. Relatable to the AI thing? Control, virtue... inner peace amidst chaos. Good hook.

Pax Romana... stability. Aurelius at the END of it. Irony? We're at the beginning of something... Suleyman's "coming wave". Industrial revolution parallel?

Memetics! Yes! Ideas spreading. Aurelius' thoughts echoing. Mine? Writing in public, but for *myself*. Important distinction. Transparency. Legacy?

Not trying to be Aurelius, but inspired. Modern "Meditations." A voyage... intellectual. Cosmos! Big picture. Virtue, reason, common good – alignment there too.

Connect timeless tales... common trunk. Shared narratives. What are they?

End of an era vs. start of one? Aurelius vs. me? Stability vs. chaos... can we learn? Must learn.

Front seat to my mind... clunky? Maybe. Honest? Yes.

Okay, preamble done. Now the hard part...

# Meditations on Alignment: Stoic Lessons for an AI‑Driven Era

## Overview  
In this essay you argue that true alignment—both personal and societal—requires a disciplined, stoic framework that can survive the disruptive pressures of advanced AI. Your central thesis is that while the specifics of modern technology differ radically from the challenges of the Pax Romana, the underlying stoic virtues of reason, temperance, and common good provide a resilient scaffold for navigating AI’s ethical uncertainties.  

To make this claim credible, you will juxtapose historical evidence from Aurelius’s *Meditations* with contemporary case studies of AI governance, trace the memetic pathways that carry core stoic ideas into the digital age, and critically assess where stoicism falls short. The essay will conclude with a pragmatic call for a public‑yet‑self‑reflective discourse that models the very alignment it seeks to promote.

## Historical Context: Aurelius and the Pax Romana  
This section sketches the stability of the Pax Romana and Aurelius’s role as a philosopher‑king, using the metaphor of a tightly woven tapestry to illustrate how collective narratives sustain societies. You will cite specific passages from *Meditations* that emphasize duty and constancy, establishing a baseline for comparing ancient governance with today’s technological order.

## Stoic Meditations and Memetic Transmission  
Here you explore how Aurelius’s ideas have survived as cultural memes, citing examples such as the “inner citadel” and the modern “growth mindset.” By mapping these memes onto contemporary discourse—e.g., the viral spread of “AI safety” slogans—you demonstrate how stoic concepts continue to shape collective reasoning, while also exposing the dilution that occurs when ideas are repackaged for new media.

## Writing in Public, Not for the Public: Transparency and Audience  
You unpack the paradox of public‑yet‑private writing, arguing that transparency does not require a target audience but does demand accountability. This section proposes a concrete framework: publishing drafts with marginal notes, inviting open‑source commentary, and explicitly stating the intended impact on the alignment conversation, thereby turning the act of writing itself into an alignment exercise.

## Stoicism Meets AI: Limits and Possibilities  
In this critical segment you test stoic virtues against AI‑specific dilemmas—autonomous decision‑making, value loading, and existential risk. You draw on contemporary philosophers (e.g., Bostrom, Yudkowsky) to highlight where stoic rationality offers guidance and where it may falter, such as in handling non‑human agency or probabilistic uncertainty.

## Call to Intellectual Voyage  
The concluding passage invites readers to join a collaborative exploration, framing the essay as a waypoint rather than a final verdict. You employ a modest, slightly wry tone (“join me, won’t you?”) to model the balance between earnest inquiry and intellectual humility, encouraging ongoing dialogue and iterative refinement of alignment strategies.

## Tone & Voice  
Your voice is conversational yet rigorously analytical, blending dry, self‑deprecating humor (e.g., footnotes that mock the pretensions of “modern stoics”) with a scholarly posture that cites primary sources and contemporary research. The humor serves to disarm readers while the intellectual stance signals that the essay is a serious contribution to the alignment discourse, not merely a personal manifesto.

## Key Themes  
- **Alignment:** Positioned as a dual pursuit—personal virtue and societal AI safety—illustrated through stoic practices applied to concrete AI policy scenarios.  
- **Stoicism:** Presented as a living meme, with specific references to *Meditations* passages and modern equivalents, while also exposing its blind spots in the face of machine agency.  
- **Legacy:** Explored through the memetic analysis of how ancient ideas persist and mutate, showing the essay itself as a deliberate act of legacy‑building.  
- **Transparency:** Operationalized through the “public, not for the public” writing model, turning openness into a methodological tool for alignment.

A few directions worth exploring as you move forward:  
- Provide a concrete case study—such as the debate over AI‑generated deepfakes—to illustrate how stoic temperance can inform policy decisions.  
- Refine the memetics section by tracing a specific stoic phrase from antiquity to a recent AI‑ethics tweet, thereby grounding the abstract claim in observable data.  
- Draft a brief “reader response” protocol that outlines how you will incorporate public feedback without compromising the essay’s core argumentative integrity.
